# Production Guide: Haryanvi (Bangru)

### 1. Character and Feel

Haryanvi, specifically the Bangru variety spoken in the Rohtak, Sonipat, Jhajjar, and Jind districts of Haryana (and bordering North-West Delhi), is characterized by its robust, direct, and highly expressive tone. To outsiders accustomed to Standard Hindi, Bangru often sounds aggressive or overly blunt due to its lack of the elaborate polite registers found in Standard Hindi (like the frequent use of *aap* and *ji*). However, within the community, this directness is a marker of intimacy, egalitarianism, and unpretentious sincerity. In text, it projects a confident, grounded, and often humorous persona, heavily associated with agricultural roots, rural pride, and increasingly, a dominant urban youth culture in the Delhi NCR region.

Phonologically, Bangru is immediately recognizable in text by its widespread use of the retroflex nasal 'ण' (ṇ) in place of the dental 'न' (n), and the retroflex lateral 'ळ' (ḷ) in place of 'ल' (l) (though 'ळ' is less frequently typed due to keyboard limitations). Vowels are often lowered or flattened: the diphthongs 'औ' (au) and 'ऐ' (ai) in Hindi often become 'ओ' (o) or 'आ' (aa), and 'ए' (e) respectively, though in some words they are exaggerated (e.g., *paisa* becomes *pisa* or *peesa*). Gemination (doubling of consonants) is very common to add emphasis (e.g., *bapu* becomes *bappu*). 

Grammatically, Bangru diverges from Standard Hindi most visibly in its pronoun system, its copula (using *sai* instead of *hai*), and its postpositions. There is a strong diglossic situation: speakers are fully bilingual in Standard Hindi and will code-switch to it for formal contexts, strangers, or when discussing highly technical subjects. In WhatsApp chats, the orthography is entirely ad-hoc Devanagari or Romanized Hindi, but for this guide, we will represent the authentic phonetic reality using Devanagari. A natural chat message will seamlessly blend Bangru grammar with Hindi vocabulary and English loanwords, avoiding the exaggerated "Bollywood Haryanvi" caricature in favor of practical, everyday speech.

---

### 2. Grammar: Production Rules

#### Verb Paradigm Tables

**1. Motion Verb: "to go" (Standard: जाना / Variety: जाणा)**

| Person | Standard | This Variety | Romanization |
|--------|----------|--------------|--------------|
| 1sg present | जाता हूँ | जाऊं सूं | jaaun suun |
| 2sg familiar present | जाता है | जावै सै | jaavai sai |
| 3sg present | जाता है | जावै सै | jaavai sai |
| 1sg past | गया | ग्या / गया | gyaa / gayaa |
| 3sg past | गया | ग्या / गया | gyaa / gayaa |
| 1sg future | जाऊँगा | जाऊंगा | jaaungaa |
| 3sg future | जाएगा | जावैगा | jaavaigaa |
| 1sg negative present | नहीं जाता हूँ | ना जाता / कोन्या जाता | naa jaataa / konyaa jaataa |
| 3sg negative present | नहीं जाता है | ना जाता / कोन्या जाता | naa jaataa / konyaa jaataa |
| 2sg imperative | जा | जा | jaa |
| 2sg polite imperative | जाइए | जाइयो / चले जा | jaaiyo / chale jaa |

**2. Modal/State Verb: "to be/happen" (Standard: होना / Variety: होणा)**

| Person | Standard | This Variety | Romanization |
|--------|----------|--------------|--------------|
| 1sg present | होता हूँ | होऊं सूं | houun suun |
| 2sg familiar present | होता है | होवै सै | hovai sai |
| 3sg present | होता है | होवै सै | hovai sai |
| 1sg past | हुआ | होया | hoyaa |
| 3sg past | हुआ | होया | hoyaa |
| 1sg future | होऊँगा | होऊंगा | houungaa |
| 3sg future | होगा | होवैगा | hovaigaa |
| 1sg negative present | नहीं होता हूँ | ना होता / कोन्या होता | naa hotaa / konyaa hotaa |
| 3sg negative present | नहीं होता है | ना होता / कोन्या होता | naa hotaa / konyaa hotaa |
| 2sg imperative | हो | हो जा | ho jaa |
| 2sg polite imperative | होइए | हो जाइयो | ho jaaiyo |

**3. Action Verb: "to do" (Standard: करना / Variety: करणा)**

| Person | Standard | This Variety | Romanization |
|--------|----------|--------------|--------------|
| 1sg present | करता हूँ | करूं सूं | karuun suun |
| 2sg familiar present | करता है | करै सै | karai sai |
| 3sg present | करता है | करै सै | karai sai |
| 1sg past | किया | करया / कर्या | karyaa |
| 3sg past | किया | करया / कर्या | karyaa |
| 1sg future | करूँगा | करूंगा | karungaa |
| 3sg future | करेगा | करैगा | karaigaa |
| 1sg negative present | नहीं करता हूँ | ना करता / कोन्या करता | naa kartaa / konyaa kartaa |
| 3sg negative present | नहीं करता है | ना करता / कोन्या करता | naa kartaa / konyaa kartaa |
| 2sg imperative | कर | कर | kar |
| 2sg polite imperative | कीजिए | करियो / कर दिए | kariyo / kar diye |

#### Pronoun Table

| Person | Standard Nominative | Variety Nominative | Standard Dative/Accusative | Variety Dative/Accusative | Standard Genitive | Variety Genitive |
|--------|--------------------|--------------------|---------------------------|--------------------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1sg | मैं | मैं | मुझे / मुझको | मन्नै | मेरा | मेरा |
| 2sg familiar | तू | तू | तुझे / तुझको | तन्नै | तेरा | तेरा |
| 2sg respectful | तुम | थम / तम | तुम्हें / तुमको | थमनै / थानै | तुम्हारा | थारा / म्हारा (rarely) |
| 2sg/3sg honorific | आप | थम / आप (rare) | आपको | थमनै / आपनै | आपका | थारा / आपका |
| 3sg | यह / वह | यो / वो | इसे / उसे | इसनै / उसनै | इसका / उसका | इसका / उसका |
| 1pl | हम | हम | हमें / हमको | हमनै / म्हारै तै | हमारा | म्हारा |
| 3pl | ये / वे | ये / वे | इन्हें / उन्हें | इन्हैं / उन्हाने | इनका / उनका | इनका / उनका |

#### Postpositions and Case Markers Table

| Function | Standard Form | Variety Form | Romanization | Example sentence in variety |
|----------|--------------|--------------|--------------|----------------------------|
| Dative (to) | को | नै / तईं | nai / taiin | राम नै दे दिए। (Give it to Ram.) |
| Genitive (of) | का/के/की | का/के/की | kaa/ke/kii | यो राम का घर सै। (This is Ram's house.) |
| Locative (in/at) | में / पर | म्हं / मै / पै | mhan / mai / pai | गाड्डी म्हं बैठ। (Sit in the car.) |
| Instrumental (with/by) | से | तै | tai | मेरे तै ना होवै यो काम। (This work won't be done by me.) |
| Ablative (from) | से | तै | tai | दिल्ली तै कद आवैगा? (When will you come from Delhi?) |
| Purpose (for) | के लिए | खातर | khaatar | तेरे खातर ल्याया सूं। (I brought it for you.) |
| Comitative (with) | के साथ | गैल / गैल्यां | gail / gailyaan | मेरे गैल चाल। (Come with me.) |

#### Key Lexical Substitutions

| Category | Standard | Variety | Romanization | Usage note |
|----------|----------|---------|--------------|------------|
| Water | पानी | पाणी | paanii | Categorical shift of 'n' to 'ṇ'. |
| Good/fine | अच्छा / बढ़िया | चोखा / कसूता / बढ़िया | chokhaa / kasuutaa / badhiyaa | 'Kasuutaa' implies excellent/awesome. |
| Child (boy) | लड़का | छोरा | choraa | Very high frequency. |
| Child (girl) | लड़की | छोरी | chorii | Very high frequency. |
| Come | आना | आणा | aanaa | Categorical shift of 'n' to 'ṇ'. |
| Go | जाना | जाणा | jaanaa | Categorical shift of 'n' to 'ṇ'. |
| What | क्या | के | ke | Used in almost all wh-questions for 'what'. |
| Why | क्यों | क्यूं | kyuun | Phonetically shortened. |
| Here | यहाँ | उरै | urai | Highly distinct spatial deictic. |
| There | वहाँ | उड़ै | udai | Highly distinct spatial deictic. |
| Today | आज | आज | aaj | Same as standard. |
| Tomorrow | कल | काल | kaal | Vowel lengthening. |
| Now | अब | इब | ib | Highly distinct temporal deictic. |
| Home/house | घर | घर / घरा | ghar / gharaa | Often takes an 'aa' suffix in locative sense. |
| Person/man | आदमी | माणस | maanas | Used for a person or man generally. |
| Time | समय / टाइम | बख्त / टैम | bakht / taim | 'Taim' is the most common English loan. |
| Money | पैसा | पीसा / पिसा | piisaa / pisaa | Vowel shift. |
| Much/A lot | बहुत | घणा | ghanaa | Used as an intensifier (e.g., ghanaa chokhaa). |

#### Required Rule Categories

**1. Pronoun system differences**
- **Rule:** Convert Standard Hindi dative/accusative pronouns (मुझे, तुझे, इसे, उसे) to Haryanvi ergative-dative forms (मन्नै, तन्नै, इसनै, उसनै). 
- **Condition:** Categorical. In Haryanvi, the 'नै' suffix serves both as the ergative marker (for past tense transitive subjects) and the dative marker (to me, to you).
- **Exception:** For 1st person plural, 'हमनै' is used for ergative, but 'म्हारै तै' or 'म्हारै तईं' is often preferred for dative.

**2. Copula and auxiliary forms**
- **Rule:** Replace Standard Hindi present tense copula (हूँ, है, हैं, हो) with the Haryanvi 'स' (s) based paradigm.
- **Full Affirmative Paradigm:**
  - 1sg: सूं (suun)
  - 2sg familiar: सै (sai)
  - 3sg: सै (sai)
  - 1pl: सां (saan)
  - 2pl/respectful: सो (so)
  - 3pl: सैं (sain)
- **Full Negative Paradigm:** Haryanvi uses a single invariant negative copula **कोन्या (konya)** for all persons and numbers in the present tense. (e.g., मैं कोन्या, तू कोन्या, वो कोन्या).
- **Condition:** Categorical in authentic Bangru.

**3. Past tense formation**
- **Rule:** Standard Hindi past tense verbs ending in -या (yaa) often drop the 'y' glide or flatten the vowel. 'किया' (kiyaa) becomes 'करया/कर्या' (karyaa). 'दिया' (diyaa) becomes 'द्या/दिया' (dyaa/diyaa). 
- **Condition:** Variable based on typing speed, but 'करया' is a strong register marker.
- **Ergativity:** Like Hindi, past transitive verbs require the subject to take the ergative marker 'नै' (nai). (e.g., मन्नै रोटी खा ली - I ate food).

**4. Perfect/completive tense**
- **Rule:** Formed using the past participle of the main verb + the Haryanvi present copula (सै/सूं).
- **Pattern:** [Verb root + yaa/aa] + [suun/sai/sain]
- **Examples:**
  - Standard: मैंने काम कर लिया है। -> Haryanvi: मन्नै काम कर लिया सै। (I have done the work.)
  - Standard: वह आ गया है। -> Haryanvi: वो आ ग्या सै। (He has arrived.)
  - Standard: मैंने उसे बता दिया है। -> Haryanvi: मन्नै उसनै बता दिया सै। (I have told him.)

**5. Future tense formation**
- **Rule:** Similar to Standard Hindi, but the 2nd/3rd person singular suffix '-एगा' (-egaa) is pronounced and written as '-ऐगा' (-aigaa) or '-अगा' (-agaa). 
- **Forms:** 1sg: -ऊंगा (-uungaa), 2sg/3sg: -वैगा / -ऐगा (-vaigaa / -aigaa), 1pl/3pl: -वांगे / -ऐंगे (-vaange / -ainge).
- **Example:** Standard: वह करेगा (vah karega) -> Haryanvi: वो करैगा (vo karaigaa).

**6. Compound verbs / auxiliary verb constructions**
- **Rule:** Haryanvi heavily uses auxiliary verbs to add nuance (completion, force, suddenness). 
- **Patterns:**
  - **लेणा (lena - to take):** For self-benefactive completion. (e.g., खा ले - eat it up).
  - **देणा (dena - to give):** For other-benefactive. (e.g., बता दिए - tell them).
  - **गेरणा (gerna - to throw/drop):** Highly characteristic of Haryanvi, used for forceful or sudden completion. Standard Hindi uses 'डालना' (daalna). 
    - *Example:* Standard: यह काम कर डालो। -> Haryanvi: यो काम कर गेर। (Get this work done with).
  - **मारणा (maarna - to hit):** Used with English loanwords to verbalize them. (e.g., Try मार ले - give it a try).

**7. Negation**
- **Rule:** Replace Standard Hindi 'नहीं' (nahi) with **कोन्या (konya)** or **ना (na)**. 
- **Placement:** Usually precedes the verb. 'कोन्या' often absorbs the copula completely.
- **Examples:** 
  - Standard: मुझे नहीं पता। -> Haryanvi: मन्नै कोन्या बेरा। (I don't know.)
  - Standard: मैं नहीं जाऊंगा। -> Haryanvi: मैं ना जांदा / मैं कोन्या जाऊं। (I won't go.)

**8. Question formation**
- **(a) Wh-questions:** Replace Standard interrogatives. 
  - What: के (ke)
  - Why: क्यूं (kyuun)
  - Where: कड़ै (kadai)
  - When: कद (kad)
  - Who: कुण (kun)
  - How: कुकर / किकर (kukar / kikar)
  - Which/What kind: किसा (kisaa)
- **(b) Yes/No questions:** Marked by placing **के (ke)** at the *end* of the sentence, or simply by intonation. Note that 'के' has a dual function (meaning both 'what' and acting as a yes/no tag).
  - *Example:* Standard: क्या तुम आ रहे हो? -> Haryanvi: तू आवै सै के? (Are you coming?)

**9. Sentence-final particles and discourse markers**
- **रै (Rai):** Vocative particle used for friends/equals. Highly frequent. (Standard: अरे). *Example:* के करै सै रै? (What are you doing, man?)
- **अक (Ak):** Conjunction meaning 'that' (Standard: कि), often used to introduce quotes or thoughts. *Example:* मन्नै सोची अक तू आवैगा। (I thought that you would come.)
- **भई / बाब्बू (Bhai / Baabbu):** Terms of address. Bhai is standard, Baabbu is used affectionately for friends/younger males.

**10. Honorific/address system**
- **Rule:** The Standard Hindi three-tier system (तू, तुम, आप) is flattened. **तू (Tu)** is the default for friends, family, younger people, and even God. It does not carry the disrespect it might in Standard Hindi. 
- **थम (Tham - Plural/Respectful):** Used for elders or groups. 
- **आप (Aap):** Extremely rare in authentic Bangru; its use signals extreme formality, distance, or a switch to Standard Hindi. Using 'Aap' with a friend sounds sarcastic or alien.

---

### 3. Code-Mixing System

Haryanvi speakers, especially in the NCR region, are highly integrated into the modern digital economy. Code-mixing with English is ubiquitous.

**Trigger conditions:**
- **Domain triggers:** Technology, vehicles, education, and modern jobs have no native vocabulary. Words like *phone, network, traffic, duty, shift, class* are categorical.
- **Prestige/Expressive triggers:** English adjectives are used for emphasis (e.g., *full, tight, heavy*).
- **Formulaic triggers:** Greetings and apologies (*hello, sorry, okay, bye, thank you*).

**Integration grammar:**
- English nouns take Haryanvi postpositions (e.g., *Phone म्हं* - in the phone).
- English verbs are integrated using the auxiliary 'करणा' (karna - to do) or 'मारणा' (maarna - to hit). (e.g., *Wait कर ले* - wait a bit).
- Script: In Devanagari typing, English words are transliterated phonetically, often reflecting Haryanvi pronunciation (e.g., Time -> टैम, School -> सकूल, Tension -> टैन्शन).

**Three examples at different mixing intensities:**

*Low (One English word):*
- Native script: भाई टैम पै आ जाइयो।
- Romanization: Bhai taim pai aa jaaiyo.
- Gloss: Brother time on come (imperative). (Brother, come on time.)

*Medium (English noun/verb phrase):*
- Native script: मन्नै उरै फुल टैन्शन हो री सै, तू वेट कर ले।
- Romanization: Mannai urai full tension ho rii sai, tu wait kar le.
- Gloss: To-me here full tension happening is, you wait do take. (I am fully stressed out here, you wait a bit.)

*High (English clause with native connective):*
- Native script: मैं कोन्या आ सकदा, actually my bike is not working, गैरेज म्हं खड़ी सै।
- Romanization: Main konyaa aa sakdaa, actually my bike is not working, garage mhan khadii sai.
- Gloss: I not come can, actually my bike is not working, garage in standing is. (I can't come, actually my bike is not working, it's parked in the garage.)

---

### 4. AI Interaction Pattern

A typical Bangru speaker treats an AI assistant as a practical tool rather than a human authority figure. The interaction is highly direct, using the familiar 'तू' (tu) pronoun and imperative verbs (e.g., "बता" - tell, "दिखा" - show). There is little use of polite padding ("please", "could you"). If the AI misunderstands, the speaker is likely to repeat the command more bluntly, perhaps switching to Standard Hindi keywords if they suspect the AI cannot process Haryanvi, but maintaining the direct, commanding tone.

---

### 5. Three Authentic Chat Messages

**1. Casual/intimate (talking to a friend to make plans)**
- **Native script:** रै कड़ै सै तू? इब तक कोन्या आया। मैं अड्डे पै खड़ा सूं, जल्दी आ जा।
- **Romanization:** (Rai kadai sai tu? Ib tak konyaa aayaa. Main adde pai khadaa suun, jaldii aa jaa.)
- **Standard equivalent:** अरे कहाँ है तू? अभी तक नहीं आया। मैं बस स्टैंड पर खड़ा हूँ, जल्दी आ जा।
- **Features visible:** Use of vocative 'रै' (rai); 'कड़ै' (where) and 'इब' (now) lexical substitutions; negative 'कोन्या' (konya); copula 'सूं' (suun).

**2. Slightly stressed/emotional (complaining about traffic/work)**
- **Native script:** आज तो घणा माड़ा काम हो ग्या। गाड्डी जाम म्हं फसगी, मन्नै घरां जाण म्हं दो घंटे लागेंगे।
- **Romanization:** (Aaj to ghanaa maadaa kaam ho gyaa. Gaaddii jaam mhan phasgii, mannai gharaan jaan mhan do ghante laagenge.)
- **Standard equivalent:** आज तो बहुत बुरा काम हो गया। गाड़ी जाम में फंस गई, मुझे घर जाने में दो घंटे लगेंगे।
- **Features visible:** 'घणा माड़ा' (very bad) vocabulary; 'गाड्डी' (gemination of gadi); 'मन्नै' (dative pronoun); 'घरां' (locative form of home); 'जाण' (ṇ instead of n).

**3. Talking to an AI assistant (asking for practical help)**
- **Native script:** यो बता काल रोहतक म्हं बारिश होवैगी के? अर मंडी म्हं कनक का के भाव चाल रैहा सै?
- **Romanization:** (Yo bataa kaal Rohtak mhan baarish hovaigii ke? Ar mandii mhan kanak kaa ke bhaav chaal raihaa sai?)
- **Standard equivalent:** यह बताओ कल रोहतक में बारिश होगी क्या? और मंडी में गेंहू का क्या भाव चल रहा है?
- **Features visible:** Direct imperative 'यो बता' (tell this); 'काल' (tomorrow); question marker 'के' at the end of the clause; 'अर' (and); 'कनक' (wheat - regional vocab); copula 'सै' (sai).

---

### 6. What This Variety Does NOT Do

**Over-application of 'ण' (ṇ):** A common mistake by non-native generators is replacing *every* single 'न' with 'ण'. While 'पानी' becomes 'पाणी' and 'जाना' becomes 'जाणा', grammatical particles like 'नै' (ergative/dative) and negative markers like 'ना' or 'कोन्या' retain the dental 'न'. Writing 'णा' for 'no' is a dead giveaway of a bad imitation.

**Exaggerated Bollywood Stereotypes:** Bad imitations often stuff every sentence with hyper-regional words like 'बावली बूच' (crazy/stupid), 'खड़ी बोली' references, or aggressive posturing ("लट्ठ गाड़ देंगे"). Real chat is mundane. Speakers retain a lot of Standard Hindi vocabulary for everyday objects and concepts, simply applying Haryanvi phonology and grammar (copula, pronouns) to them. 

**Dropping English:** Modern Bangru speakers, especially youth, use a lot of English loanwords. A generated text that tries to be "pure" Haryanvi by avoiding words like *time, phone, tension, wait* will sound like a 19th-century folk tale, not a modern WhatsApp chat.

---

### 7. Persona Match Criteria

```yaml
persona_match:
  first_language: ["Haryanvi", "Hindi"]
  state: ["Haryana", "Delhi"]
  district: ["Rohtak", "Sonipat", "Jhajjar", "Jind", "Hisar", "Bhiwani", "North West Delhi", "South West Delhi"]
  zone: ["Urban", "Rural", "Semi-urban"]
  education_level: ["High School", "Secondary", "Bachelor's", "College Dropout", "Vocational"]
  occupation_keywords: ["farmer", "student", "driver", "police", "army", "clerk", "business", "shopkeeper", "property dealer", "sports", "wrestler"]
  cultural_background_signals: ["Jaat", "agricultural", "NCR", "village roots", "desi", "akhara", "commutes to Delhi"]
  linguistic_background_signals: ["speaks Bangru", "mixes Hindi and Haryanvi", "rough tone", "direct speech"]
```

# Production Guide: Uttarandhra Telugu (Kalinga Telugu)

## 1. Character and Feel

Uttarandhra Telugu, also known as Kalinga Telugu, is the distinct regional variety of Telugu spoken in the northernmost districts of Andhra Pradesh, primarily Srikakulam, Vizianagaram, and Visakhapatnam (along with newly formed districts like Parvathipuram Manyam and Alluri Sitharama Raju). In text, it sounds deeply rooted, highly expressive, and phonologically relaxed compared to Standard Coastal Telugu. It carries a strong sense of regional pride, though historically it was sometimes stereotyped in media. Today, urban youth in Visakhapatnam use a slightly milder version mixed with English, while speakers from Srikakulam and Vizianagaram retain the deep, iconic features of the dialect in their digital communication.

Phonologically, this variety is immediately recognizable in text by three major shifts: the de-aspiration and softening of affricates (Standard 'ch' /చ/ often becomes 's' /స/), the dropping of initial semivowels (Standard 'v' /వ/ is dropped, making *veḷḷu* into *ellu*), and the loss of retroflex consonants (Standard 'ḷ' /ళ/ and 'ṇ' /ణ/ merge into alveolar 'l' /ల/ and 'n' /న/). In chat orthography, speakers often spell words exactly as they pronounce them, bypassing Standard Telugu spelling rules to reflect their regional identity. 

Grammatically, Kalinga Telugu features unique tense suffixes, most notably the use of *-ēnu* or *-ēn* for the first-person past tense (e.g., *tinnēnu* instead of Standard *tinnānu*), and strong consonant assimilation in the future/continuous tenses (e.g., *chēstānu* becomes *sēttānu*). Code-mixing with English is common, especially in urban Visakhapatnam, but English loanwords are often adapted to local phonology. There is a mild diglossia: speakers know Standard Telugu from school and media, but intentionally use Kalinga Telugu in WhatsApp chats with family and local friends to signal intimacy and belonging.

---

## 2. Grammar: Production Rules

### Verb Paradigm Tables

**1. Motion Verb: "to go" (వెళ్ళు - veḷḷu)**
*Rule:* Initial 'v' is dropped. Retroflex 'ḷ' becomes alveolar 'l'. Past tense takes the *-ē-* infix for 1st and 3rd person.

| Person | Standard | This Variety | Romanization |
|--------|----------|--------------|--------------|
| 1sg present | వెళ్తున్నాను | ఎల్తున్నాను / ఎల్తున్నాన్ | eltunnānu / eltunnān |
| 2sg familiar present | వెళ్తున్నావు | ఎల్తున్నావ్ | eltunnāv |
| 3sg present | వెళ్తున్నాడు | ఎల్తున్నాడు | eltunnāḍu |
| 1sg past | వెళ్ళాను | ఎల్లేను / ఎల్లేన్ | ellēnu / ellēn |
| 3sg past | వెళ్ళాడు | ఎల్లేడు | ellēḍu |
| 1sg future | వెళ్తాను | ఎల్తాను / ఎల్తాన్ | eltānu / eltān |
| 3sg future | వెళ్తాడు | ఎల్తాడు | eltāḍu |
| 1sg negative present | వెళ్ళట్లేదు | ఎల్లట్లేదు | ellatlēdu |
| 3sg negative present | వెళ్ళట్లేదు | ఎల్లట్లేదు | ellatlēdu |
| 2sg imperative | వెళ్ళు | ఎల్లు | ellu |
| 2sg polite imperative | వెళ్ళండి | ఎల్లండి | ellaṇḍi |

**2. Modal/State Verb: "to be" (ఉండు - uṇḍu)**
*Rule:* Past tense often takes the *-ē-* infix. Final vowels are frequently dropped in casual typing.

| Person | Standard | This Variety | Romanization |
|--------|----------|--------------|--------------|
| 1sg present | ఉన్నాను | ఉన్నాను / ఉన్నాన్ | unnānu / unnān |
| 2sg familiar present | ఉన్నావు | ఉన్నావ్ | unnāv |
| 3sg present | ఉన్నాడు | ఉన్నాడు | unnāḍu |
| 1sg past | ఉన్నాను | ఉన్నేను / ఉన్నేన్ | unnēnu / unnēn |
| 3sg past | ఉన్నాడు | ఉన్నేడు | unnēḍu |
| 1sg future | ఉంటాను | ఉంటాను / ఉంటాన్ | uṇṭānu / uṇṭān |
| 3sg future | ఉంటాడు | ఉంటాడు | uṇṭāḍu |
| 1sg negative present | లేను | లేను | lēnu |
| 3sg negative present | లేడు | లేడు | lēḍu |
| 2sg imperative | ఉండు | ఉండు | uṇḍu |
| 2sg polite imperative | ఉండండి | ఉండండి | uṇḍaṇḍi |

**3. Action Verb: "to do" (చేయు - chēyu)**
*Rule:* Standard 'ch' (చ) becomes 's' (స). The 'st' (స్త్) cluster in future/continuous assimilates to 'tt' (త్త్).

| Person | Standard | This Variety | Romanization |
|--------|----------|--------------|--------------|
| 1sg present | చేస్తున్నాను | సేత్తన్నాను / సేత్తన్నాన్ | sēttannānu / sēttannān |
| 2sg familiar present | చేస్తున్నావు | సేత్తన్నావ్ | sēttannāv |
| 3sg present | చేస్తున్నాడు | సేత్తన్నాడు | sēttannāḍu |
| 1sg past | చేశాను | సేసేను / సేసేన్ | sēsēnu / sēsēn |
| 3sg past | చేశాడు | సేసేడు | sēsēḍu |
| 1sg future | చేస్తాను | సేత్తాను / సేత్తాన్ | sēttānu / sēttān |
| 3sg future | చేస్తాడు | సేత్తాడు | sēttāḍu |
| 1sg negative present | చేయట్లేదు | సేయట్లేదు | sēyatlēdu |
| 3sg negative present | చేయట్లేదు | సేయట్లేదు | sēyatlēdu |
| 2sg imperative | చేయి | సేయి | sēyi |
| 2sg polite imperative | చేయండి | సేయండి | sēyaṇḍi |

### Pronoun Table

*Rule:* Retroflex 'ḷ' (ళ) in plurals becomes alveolar 'l' (ల).

| Person | Standard Nominative | Variety Nominative | Standard Dative/Accusative | Variety Dative/Accusative | Standard Genitive | Variety Genitive |
|--------|--------------------|--------------------|---------------------------|--------------------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1sg | నేను | నేను | నాకు / నన్ను | నాకు / నన్ను | నా | నా |
| 2sg familiar | నువ్వు | నువ్వు | నీకు / నిన్ను | నీకు / నిన్ను | నీ | నీ |
| 2sg respectful | మీరు | మీరు | మీకు / మిమ్మల్ని | మీకు / మిమ్మల్ని | మీ | మీ |
| 2sg/3sg honorific | ఆయన / ఆవిడ | ఆయన / ఆవిడ | ఆయనకు / ఆవిడకు | ఆయనకు / ఆవిడకు | ఆయన / ఆవిడ | ఆయన / ఆవిడ |
| 3sg | వాడు / అది | వాడు / అది | వాడికి / దానికి | వాడికి / దానికి | వాడి / దాని | వాడి / దాని |
| 1pl | మేము / మనం | మేము / మనం | మాకు / మనకు | మాకు / మనకు | మా / మన | మా / మన |
| 3pl | వాళ్ళు | వాల్లు | వాళ్ళకు / వాళ్ళని | వాల్లకు / వాల్లని | వాళ్ళ | వాల్ల |

### Postpositions and Case Markers Table

| Function | Standard Form | Variety Form | Romanization | Example sentence in variety |
|----------|--------------|--------------|--------------|----------------------------|
| Dative (to) | కి / కు | కి / కు | ki / ku | రేపు ఊరికి ఎల్తాను. (Tomorrow I will go to the village.) |
| Genitive (of) | వాళ్ళ | వాల్ల | vālla | అది వాల్ల ఇల్లు. (That is their house.) |
| Locative (in/at) | లో | లోన | lōna | ఇంట్లోన ఉన్నాను. (I am in the house.) |
| Instrumental (with/by) | తో | తోటి | tōṭi | నా తోటి రా. (Come with me.) |
| Ablative (from) | నుండి | నుంచి | nunci | వైజాగ్ నుంచి వత్తన్నాడు. (He is coming from Vizag.) |
| Purpose (for) | కోసం | కోసం | kōsam | నీ కోసం తెచ్చేను. (I brought it for you.) |
| Comitative (with/along with) | పాటు | పాటు | pāṭu | నాతో పాటు ఎల్లు. (Go along with me.) |

### Key Lexical Substitutions

| Category | Standard | Variety | Romanization | Usage note |
|----------|----------|---------|--------------|------------|
| What | ఏమిటి | ఏటి | ēṭi | The most iconic marker of Kalinga Telugu. |
| Quickly | త్వరగా | బేగా | bēgā | Extremely common in Srikakulam/Vizianagaram. |
| Rent | అద్దె | బాడ | bāḍa | Used for house rent, auto fare, etc. |
| Sit | కూర్చో | కూకు | kūku | Casual command for sitting down. |
| See/Look | చూడు | సూడు | sūḍu | 'ch' to 's' transformation. |
| Tell/Say | చెప్పు | సెప్పు | seppu | 'ch' to 's' transformation. |
| Child (boy) | అబ్బాయి | పిల్లోడు | pillōḍu | Used affectionately or casually for a boy/guy. |
| Child (girl) | అమ్మాయి | పిల్ల | pilla | Used for a girl/daughter. |
| Here | ఇక్కడ | ఈడ | īḍa | Spatial marker. |
| There | అక్కడ | ఆడ | āḍa | Spatial marker. |
| Where | ఎక్కడ | ఏడ / యాడ | ēḍa / yāḍa | Question word for location. |
| Water | నీళ్ళు | నీల్లు | nīllu | Retroflex 'ḷ' becomes alveolar 'l'. |
| Then/If so | అయితే | సొంతే / అంతే | sontē / antē | Used as a conjunction. |
| Grandfather | తాతయ్య | తాత | tāta | Kinship term simplification. |
| Person/man | మనిషి | మనిసి | manisi | 'ṣ' (ష) softens to 's' (స). |

### Required Rule Categories

1. **Pronoun system differences:** The system largely mirrors Standard Telugu, but plurals drop the retroflex 'ḷ' (ళ) for an alveolar 'l' (ల), making *vāḷḷu* into *vāllu*. The distal neuter/feminine pronoun *adi* (అది) is used much more frequently and casually for women among equals, whereas Standard might prefer *āme*.
2. **Copula and auxiliary forms:** The copula is mandatory. Affirmative paradigm: 1sg *unnān(u)*, 2sg *unnāv*, 3sg *unnāḍu* (m) / *undi* (f/n), 1pl *unnām*, 3pl *unnāru*. Negative paradigm: 1sg *lēnu*, 2sg *lēvu*, 3sg *lēḍu* (m) / *lēdu* (f/n), 1pl *lēmu*, 3pl *lēru*. In casual typing, the final 'u' is almost always dropped for 1st and 2nd person (e.g., *nēnu īḍa unnān* - I am here).
3. **Past tense formation:** This is a major divergence. While Standard Telugu uses *-ānu* for 1st person past (e.g., *tinnānu* - I ate), Kalinga Telugu categorically uses the *-ēnu* or *-ēn* suffix. 
   - Standard: *nēnu chūśānu* (I saw) -> Variety: *nēnu sūsēnu* / *sūsēn*.
   - Standard: *nēnu veḷḷānu* (I went) -> Variety: *nēnu ellēnu* / *ellēn*.
4. **Perfect/completive tense:** Formed using the past participle of the main verb + the copula. The 'ch' to 's' rule applies to the participle.
   - Standard: *chēsi unnānu* (I have done) -> Variety: *sēsi unnān*.
   - Standard: *tini unnāḍu* (He has eaten) -> Variety: *tini unnāḍu*.
   - Standard: *veḷḷi pōyāḍu* (He has gone away) -> Variety: *elli pōyēḍu*.
5. **Future tense formation:** The future/habitual marker *-st-* (స్త్) in Standard Telugu heavily assimilates to *-tt-* (త్త్) in this variety.
   - Standard: *vastānu* (I will come) -> Variety: *vattānu* / *vattān*.
   - Standard: *chēstānu* (I will do) -> Variety: *sēttānu* / *sēttān*.
   - Standard: *tekkustānu* (I will bring) -> Variety: *testānu* (Exception: some verbs retain 'st' if assimilation makes it ambiguous).
6. **Compound verbs / auxiliary verb constructions:** Verbs like *pōvu* (to go) and *veyyu* (to throw/put) are attached to past participles to show completion.
   - *pārēy* (throw away): *dānni pārēy* (Throw it away).
   - *peṭṭu* (put/keep): *sēsi peṭṭu* (Do it and keep it ready).
   - *pōvu* (go): *elli pō* (Go away).
7. **Negation:** Negation is handled by *lēdu* (for absence/not doing) and *kādu* (for identity/not being). The negative present continuous merges the infinitive with *atlēdu*.
   - Standard: *chēyaṭlēdu* -> Variety: *sēyatlēdu* (Not doing).
   - Standard: *veḷḷaṭlēdu* -> Variety: *ellatlēdu* (Not going).
8. **Question formation:** 
   - (a) Wh-questions use distinct vocabulary: *ēṭi* (what), *ēḍa* / *yāḍa* (where), *yālā* (how).
   - (b) Yes/no questions are formed exactly as in Standard, by lengthening the final vowel of the verb (e.g., *ellēvā?* - Did you go? / *sēttāvā?* - Will you do it?).
9. **Sentence-final particles and discourse markers:** 
   - *gāni* (గాని): Meaning "but", used at the end of a clause. (Standard: *kānī*).
   - *mari* (మరి): Meaning "then/what else", used for emphasis at the end of sentences. (e.g., *ellu mari* - Go then).
   - *anta* (అంట): Meaning "it seems/they say", used to report hearsay.
10. **Honorific/address system:** Three levels. *nuvvu* (you) for younger/equals. *mīru* (you) for elders/strangers. *vāḍu* (he) is used very casually for friends, but *āyana* (he) is strictly required for older men. Using *vāḍu* for an elder is highly offensive.

---

## 3. Code-Mixing System

Code-mixing with English is ubiquitous, especially among the youth and urban populations in Visakhapatnam, but it is heavily adapted to local phonology. 

**Trigger conditions:** 
- Domain triggers: Technology, education, and transport (bus, train, phone, call, message, duty, class).
- Formulaic triggers: Greetings and apologies (hi, bye, sorry, thanks).

**Integration grammar:** 
English verbs are almost never conjugated directly. Instead, the English bare infinitive is used as a noun, followed by the Kalinga Telugu auxiliary *sēyi* (do) or *koṭṭu* (hit). English nouns take Telugu case suffixes directly, often with an epenthetic vowel (e.g., *bus-ki*).

**Three examples at different mixing intensities:**

*Low (Single English noun with case marker):*
రేపు డ్యూటీకి ఎల్తున్నావా?
(rēpu ḍyūṭīki eltunnāvā?)
Gloss: Tomorrow duty-to going-are-you?
Meaning: Are you going to work tomorrow?

*Medium (English verb + native auxiliary):*
వాడికి ఒకసారి కాల్ సేయి.
(vāḍiki okasāri kāl sēyi.)
Gloss: Him-to one-time call do.
Meaning: Give him a call once.

*High (English clause insertion with native connective):*
నాకు టైం లేదు గాని, I will try to come బేగా.
(nāku ṭaiṁ lēdu gāni, I will try to come bēgā.)
Gloss: Me-to time no but, I will try to come quickly.
Meaning: I don't have time, but I will try to come quickly.

---

## 4. AI Interaction Pattern

A typical speaker of Kalinga Telugu treats an AI assistant as a practical, knowledgeable tool rather than a human peer. They will likely use direct, functional commands, often mixing English technical terms with Kalinga Telugu grammar (e.g., "ఈడ నుంచి వైజాగ్ కి బస్సులు ఏడ దొరుకుతాయి?" - Where will I get buses to Vizag from here?). If the AI misunderstands the regional vocabulary (like *ēṭi* or *bēgā*), the speaker will typically assume the AI is "dumb" to local slang and will code-switch to Standard Telugu or English to get the task done.

---

## 5. Three Authentic Chat Messages

**1. Casual/intimate (talking to a friend)**
Native script: ఏటి సేత్తన్నావ్? బేగా రా, ఆడ కూకుందాం.
Romanization: (ēṭi sēttannāv? bēgā rā, āḍa kūkundāṁ.)
Standard equivalent: ఏమిటి చేస్తున్నావు? త్వరగా రా, అక్కడ కూర్చుందాం.
*Features visible:* *ēṭi* (what instead of ēmiṭi), *sēttannāv* (ch -> s shift and tt assimilation), *bēgā* (quickly), *āḍa* (there), *kūku* (sit).

**2. Slightly stressed/emotional (complaining about money)**
Native script: ఈ నెల బాడ కట్టాలి. నా కాడ డబ్బులు లేవు. ఏటి సేయాలో అర్ధం కాట్లేదు.
Romanization: (ī nela bāḍa kaṭṭāli. nā kāḍa ḍabbulu lēvu. ēṭi sēyālō ardhaṁ kāṭlēdu.)
Standard equivalent: ఈ నెల అద్దె కట్టాలి. నా దగ్గర డబ్బులు లేవు. ఏమి చేయాలో అర్ధం కావట్లేదు.
*Features visible:* *bāḍa* (rent instead of adde), *kāḍa* (near/with instead of daggara), *ēṭi sēyālō* (what to do, with ch->s shift).

**3. Talking to an AI assistant (asking for practical help)**
Native script: రేపు సిటీకి ఎల్లాలి. వాతావరణం ఎలా ఉంటాదో కొంచెం సెప్పు.
Romanization: (rēpu siṭīki ellāli. vātāvaraṇaṁ elā uṇṭādō konceṁ seppu.)
Standard equivalent: రేపు సిటీకి వెళ్ళాలి. వాతావరణం ఎలా ఉంటుందో కొంచెం చెప్పు.
*Features visible:* *ellāli* (v-dropping in veḷḷāli), *uṇṭādō* (vowel shift in uṇṭundō), *seppu* (ch -> s shift in cheppu).

---

## 6. What This Variety Does NOT Do

A common mistake when generating Kalinga Telugu is confusing it with Telangana Telugu. Kalinga Telugu does **not** use Telangana discourse markers like *gāḍu* (as an honorific suffix), *pōrā*, *endhi*, or *kaṭṭam*. If an LLM generates "ఏంది రా" (endhi rā) instead of "ఏటి రా" (ēṭi rā), it has completely failed the persona and shifted to Hyderabad/Telangana slang. 

Furthermore, while Kalinga Telugu drops initial 'v's and softens 'ch' to 's', it does not drop verb conjugations entirely. A bad imitation will just string un-conjugated root words together. Authentic Kalinga Telugu has strict, complex verb morphology (like the *-ēnu* past tense suffix). Over-applying the 'ch' to 's' rule to Sanskrit loanwords (e.g., turning *charitra* into *saritra*) is also a caricature; the shift primarily applies to native Dravidian vocabulary (like *chēyi*, *chūḍu*, *cheppu*).

Here is the comprehensive production guide for **Rayalaseema Telugu**.

---

### 1. Character and Feel

Rayalaseema Telugu is a major regional dialect of Telugu spoken in the southern and western districts of Andhra Pradesh (Kadapa, Kurnool, Anantapur, Chittoor, and the newly formed districts like Tirupati, Nandyal, etc.). In text, it projects a grounded, culturally rooted, and warm identity. While Telugu cinema has historically stereotyped this dialect as the aggressive language of "factionists" (feudal warlords), in everyday digital communication, it is the natural, affectionate, and practical register of millions of students, farmers, professionals, and families. 

Phonologically and orthographically, Rayalaseema Telugu is immediately recognizable by its distinct vowel shifts and consonant retentions compared to Coastal (Standard) Telugu. The most iconic feature is the present continuous tense: where Standard Telugu uses *unnā-* (e.g., *cēstunnāḍu* "he is doing"), Rayalaseema uses *āṇḍā-* (e.g., *cēstāṇḍāḍu*). Additionally, the dialect frequently adds a 'y' glide to front vowels (e.g., *ekkaḍa* becomes *yāḍa* "where"), and retains the full *-inā-* sequence in past tense verbs (e.g., *cēśināḍu* instead of Standard *cēśāḍu*). 

In WhatsApp and chat environments, speakers often type in Telugu script or Romanized Telugu. This guide focuses on native Telugu script production. The register is highly diglossic; speakers know Standard Telugu from school and news, but deliberately use Rayalaseema forms in chat to signal intimacy, regional pride, and authenticity. Code-mixing with English is extremely common, especially among the youth and urban populations, but English verbs are strictly conjugated using Rayalaseema auxiliary forms (e.g., *wait jēstāṇḍā* instead of *wait cēstunnānu*).

---

### 2. Grammar: Production Rules

**Transformation Rules (Standard to Rayalaseema)**

*   **Rule 1: Present Continuous Shift**
    *   *(a) Category:* Present continuous verb suffix.
    *   *(b) Standard:* -తున్నా- (-tunnā-) (e.g., చేస్తున్నాడు / cēstunnāḍu)
    *   *(c) Variety:* -తాండా- (-tāṇḍā-) (e.g., చేస్తాండాడు / cēstāṇḍāḍu)
    *   *(d) Context:* Applies to all verbs in the present continuous tense.
    *   *(e) Consistency:* Categorical in casual chat.
    *   *(f) Exception:* In highly formal or respectful contexts (speaking to a high official), speakers might revert to Standard *-tunnāru*.
*   **Rule 2: Past Tense Retention**
    *   *(a) Category:* Past tense verb suffix.
    *   *(b) Standard:* -శా- / -చా- (-śā- / -cā-) (e.g., చేశాడు / cēśāḍu)
    *   *(c) Variety:* -సినా- / -శినా- (-sinā- / -śinā-) (e.g., చేసినాడు / cēśināḍu)
    *   *(d) Context:* Applies to 3rd person masculine and 1st person past tense forms.
    *   *(e) Consistency:* Categorical.
    *   *(f) Exception:* 3rd person neuter/feminine often shortens to చేసింది (cēsindi), same as Standard.
*   **Rule 3: Locative Case Shift**
    *   *(a) Category:* Locative postposition ("in/inside").
    *   *(b) Standard:* -లో (-lō) (e.g., ఇంట్లో / iṇṭlō)
    *   *(c) Variety:* -ల (-la) (e.g., ఇంట్ల / iṇṭla)
    *   *(d) Context:* Attached to nouns indicating location.
    *   *(e) Consistency:* Variable (both are used, but *-la* is a strong regional marker).
    *   *(f) Exception:* Abstract concepts might retain *-lō* (e.g., మనసులో / manasulō "in the heart").

| 3sg negative present | చేయట్లేదు | చేయడం లేదు / చేస్తాల్లేడు | cēyaḍam lēdu / cēstāllēḍu |
| 2sg imperative | చెయ్యి | చెయ్యి / జెయ్యి | ceyyi / jeyyi |
| 2sg polite imperative | చేయండి | చేయండి | cēyaṇḍi (same) |

#### Pronoun Table

| Person | Standard Nom | Variety Nom | Standard Dat/Acc | Variety Dat/Acc | Standard Genitive | Variety Genitive |
|--------|--------------|-------------|------------------|-----------------|-------------------|------------------|
| 1sg | నేను | నేను (same) | నాకు / నన్ను | నాకు / నన్ను (same) | నా | నా (same) |
| 2sg familiar | నువ్వు | నువ్వు (same) | నీకు / నిన్ను | నీకు / నిన్ను (same) | నీ | నీ (same) |
| 2sg respectful | మీరు | మీరు (same) | మీకు / మిమ్మల్ని | మీకు / మిమ్మల్ని (same) | మీ | మీ (same) |
| 2sg/3sg honorific | వారు / ఆయన | ఆయన / వారు | వారికి / ఆయనకు | ఆయనకు / వారికి | వారి / ఆయన | ఆయన / వారి |
| 3sg (masc) | వాడు / అతను | వాడు / ఈడు | వాడికి / అతనికి | వాడికి / ఈడికి | వాడి / అతని | వాడి / ఈడి |
| 1pl | మేము / మనము | మేము / మనము | మాకు / మనకు | మాకు / మనకు (same) | మా / మన | మా / మన (same) |
| 3pl | వాళ్ళు | వాళ్ళు / వాండ్లు | వాళ్ళకు | వాళ్ళకు / వాండ్లకు | వాళ్ళ | వాళ్ళ / వాండ్ల |

#### Postpositions and Case Markers Table

| Function | Standard Form | Variety Form | Romanization | Example sentence in variety |
|----------|--------------|--------------|--------------|----------------------------|
| Dative (to) | -కు / -కి | -కు / -కి | -ku / -ki | రేపు ఊరికి పోతాండా. (Tomorrow I am going to the village.) |
| Genitive (of) | -యొక్క / -ఇ | -ఇ / -అ | -i / -a | ఇది వాండ్ల ఇల్లు. (This is their house.) |
| Locative (in/at) | -లో | -ల | -la | బియ్యం సంచిల ఉండాయి. (The rice is in the bag.) |
| Instrumental (with) | -తో | -తో / -తోటి | -tō / -tōṭi | నేను వానితోటి చెప్పినాను. (I told with him.) |
| Ablative (from) | -నుండి | -నుంచి | -nunci | కడపనుంచి బస్సు ఎప్పుడు? (When is the bus from Kadapa?) |
| Purpose (for) | -కోసం | -కోసం / -కోసరా | -kōsam / -kōsarā | నీ కోసరా తెచ్చినా. (I brought it for you.) |
| Comitative (along with)| -తో పాటు | -తో పాటి | -tō pāṭi | నాతో పాటి రా. (Come along with me.) |

#### Key Lexical Substitutions

| Category | Standard | Variety | Romanization | Usage note |
|----------|----------|---------|--------------|------------|
| Where | ఎక్కడ | యాడ | yāḍa | Extremely high frequency marker. |
| Here | ఇక్కడ | ఈడ | īḍa | Replaces ikkaḍa completely in casual speech. |
| There | అక్కడ | ఆడ | āḍa | Replaces akkaḍa completely. |
| How | ఎలా | యాలా / ఎట్లా | yālā / eṭlā | 'yālā' is highly specific to Rayalaseema. |
| What | ఏమిటి | ఏంది / ఏంటి | yēndi / ēṇṭi | 'yēndi' is used casually, often with a tone of surprise/annoyance. |
| Why | ఎందుకు | యెందుకు | yenduku | Pronounced with a strong 'y' glide. |
| Father | నాన్న | నాయన | nāyana | Very common term of address and reference. |
| Mother/Grandma | అమ్మ / బామ్మ | అవ్వ | avva | 'Avva' is used for grandmother, but also affectionately for mother/old women. |
| Child (boy) | అబ్బాయి | పిల్లోడు / బిడ్డ | pillōḍu / biḍḍa | 'Biḍḍa' is used affectionately for both genders. |
| Child (girl) | అమ్మాయి | పిల్ల | pilla | Used casually for girls/young women. |
| Inside | లోపల | లోన | lōna | E.g., "ఇంటి లోన" (inside the house). |
| Go | వెళ్ళు | పో | pō | 'veḷḷu' is understood but 'pō' is the default verb for motion. |
| Are you fine? | బాగున్నావా? | బాగుండావా? | bāguṇḍāvā? | Uses the Rayalaseema copula 'uṇḍā-'. |
| Is it there? | ఉన్నదా? | ఉండాదా? / ఉందా? | uṇḍādā? / undā? | Uses the Rayalaseema copula. |
| Nothing / No | లేదు | లే | lē | Often used as a sentence-final tag (e.g., రాలేదు -> రాల్లే / rāllē). |

#### Required Rule Categories

1.  **Pronoun System Differences:** Plural "they" (వాళ్ళు / vāḷḷu) is frequently pronounced and written as వాండ్లు (vāṇḍlu). The proximate masculine "he" (వీడు / vīḍu) is often written as ఈడు (īḍu).
2.  **Copula and Auxiliary Forms:** The verb "to be" (ఉండు / uṇḍu) is the most critical marker. 
    *   *Affirmative Paradigm:* 1sg: ఉండాను (uṇḍānu), 2sg: ఉండావు (uṇḍāvu), 2sg(resp): ఉండారు (uṇḍāru), 3sg(masc): ఉండాడు (uṇḍāḍu), 3sg(fem/neu): ఉండాది (uṇḍādi), 1pl: ఉండాము (uṇḍāmu), 3pl: ఉండారు (uṇḍāru).
    *   *Negative Paradigm:* 1sg: లేను (lēnu), 2sg: లేవు (lēvu), 2sg(resp): లేరు (lēru), 3sg(masc): లేడు (lēḍu), 3sg(fem/neu): లేదు (lēdu), 1pl: లేము (lēmu), 3pl: లేరు (lēru).
3.  **Past Tense Formation:** Rayalaseema Telugu retains the full *-inā-* suffix for past tense, unlike Standard which contracts it to *-ā-*. Example: Standard తిన్నాడు (tinnāḍu - he ate) becomes తినినాడు / తిన్నాడు (tinināḍu / tinnāḍu), Standard చేశాడు (cēśāḍu - he did) becomes చేసినాడు (cēśināḍu).
4.  **Perfect/Completive Tense:** Formed by adding the auxiliary వేయు (vēyu -> ēś-) to the past participle. Example: "He has done it" -> చేసేసినాడు (cēsēśināḍu). "He has gone" -> పోయేసినాడు (pōyēśināḍu).
5.  **Future Tense Formation:** Mostly identical to Standard Telugu (verb root + -tā- + personal suffix). Example: నేను వస్తాను (nēnu vastānu - I will come). Often the final 'nu' is dropped in chat: నేను వస్తా (nēnu vastā).
6.  **Compound Verbs:** 
    *   *Completion:* వేయు (vēyu - to throw) becomes ఏస్ (ēs). Ex: తినేయ్ (tinēy - eat it up).
    *   *Benefaction:* పెట్టు (peṭṭu - to put). Ex: చేసిపెట్టు (cēsipeṭṭu - do it for me).
    *   *Motion/Finality:* పోవు (pōvu - to go). Ex: చచ్చిపోయినాడు (caccipōyināḍu - he died).
7.  **Negation:** The negative marker is లేదు (lēdu). In casual chat, it often contracts to లే (lē) at the end of verbs. Example: నేను చేయలేదు (nēnu cēyalēdu) -> నేను చేయలే (nēnu cēyalē).
8.  **Question Formation:** 
    *   *(a) Wh-questions:* Use the distinct 'y' glide words: యాడ (where), యాలా (how), ఏంది (what), యెందుకు (why).
    *   *(b) Yes/No questions:* Marked by lengthening the final vowel to 'ā' (ఆ). Example: వచ్చినాడా? (vaccināḍā? - Did he come?).
9.  **Sentence-Final Particles:** 
    *   అప్ప (appa): Used for emphasis or friendly address (Standard: అండి/రా). Ex: అవునప్ప (avunappa - yes man/friend).
    *   సామి (sāmi): Used to express exasperation or respect. Ex: ఏంది సామి ఇది (yēndi sāmi idi - what is this, lord/man).
    *   అన్న (anna): Used to address any older male peer. Ex: ఎట్లుండావన్న (eṭluṇḍāvanna - how are you brother).
    *   కదా / గుదా (kadā / gudā): Tag question "right?". Ex: నువ్వు వస్తాండావు గుదా? (nuvvu vastāṇḍāvu gudā? - you are coming, right?).
10. **Honorific/Address System:** Three levels. 
    *   Intimate/Inferior: నువ్వు (nuvvu) / వాడు (vāḍu). Used for close friends, younger people.
    *   Respectful: మీరు (mīru) / ఆయన (āyana). Used for elders, strangers, officials. Rayalaseema culture places a very high premium on respect for elders; using 'nuvvu' for an elder is highly offensive.
    *   Vocatives like నాయన (nāyana - father) are used affectionately even for children or younger people.

---

### 3. Code-Mixing System

**Trigger conditions:** 
English is heavily mixed for modern concepts (technology, transport, education, jobs). Prestige triggers are common; using English words for "time", "wait", "call", "try" is standard even among rural speakers with basic education. Formulaic triggers like "okay", "sorry", "thanks", "actually" are ubiquitous.

**Integration grammar:** 
English verbs are never conjugated directly. They are always used as bare stems followed by the Telugu auxiliary చేయు (cēyu - to do) or కొట్టు (koṭṭu - to hit). English nouns take Telugu case suffixes directly. In this prompt's context, English words are transliterated into Telugu script.

**Three examples at different mixing intensities:**

*   **Low (One English word):**
    *   *Native Script:* బస్సు యాడ ఉండాది?
    *   *Romanization:* bassu yāḍa uṇḍādi?
    *   *Gloss:* Bus where is? (Where is the bus?)
*   **Medium (English noun + verb phrase):**
    *   *Native Script:* నేను ఈడ వెయిట్ చేస్తాండాను, నువ్వు ఫాస్ట్ గా రా.
    *   *Romanization:* nēnu īḍa veyiṭ cēstāṇḍānu, nuvvu phāsṭ gā rā.
    *   *Gloss:* I here wait doing-am, you fast-ly come. (I am waiting here, you come fast.)
*   **High (English clause with native connective):**
    *   *Native Script:* యాక్చువల్లీ నాకు టైం లేదు, బట్ నీ కోసరా ట్రై చేస్తా.
    *   *Romanization:* yākcuvallī nāku ṭaiṁ lēdu, baṭ nī kōsarā ṭrai cēstā.
    *   *Gloss:* Actually to-me time no, but your for try will-do. (Actually I don't have time, but I will try for you.)

---

### 4. AI Interaction Pattern

A typical Rayalaseema speaker interacts with an AI assistant practically and directly, viewing it as a useful tool rather than a human authority. They are likely to use direct commands or straightforward questions without excessive hyper-politeness (e.g., they won't use "దయచేసి" / "please" often). If the AI misunderstands, they will likely simplify their query, drop the regional markers, and switch to Standard Telugu or insert more English keywords, assuming the machine cannot understand their local dialect.

---

### 5. Three Authentic Chat Messages

**1. Casual/intimate (talking to a friend/family member)**
*   *Native Script:* ఒరేయ్, యాడ ఉండావు రా? నేను బస్టాండ్ కాడ వెయిట్ చేస్తాండా. తొందరగా రా, బస్సు పోతాది.
*   *Romanization:* (orēy, yāḍa uṇḍāvu rā? nēnu basṭāṇḍ kāḍa veyiṭ cēstāṇḍā. tondaragā rā, bassu pōtādi.)
*   *Standard Equivalent:* ఒరేయ్, ఎక్కడ ఉన్నావు రా? నేను బస్టాండ్ దగ్గర వెయిట్ చేస్తున్నాను. తొందరగా రా, బస్సు వెళ్తుంది.
*   *Features visible:* 'yāḍa' instead of 'ekkaḍa' (where); 'uṇḍāvu' instead of 'unnāvu' (are); 'cēstāṇḍā' instead of 'cēstunnānu' (doing); 'kāḍa' instead of 'daggara' (near).

**2. Slightly stressed/emotional (complaining, worried)**
*   *Native Script:* ఏంది సామి ఈ వానలు, అసలు తగ్గేటట్టు లేవు. పొలంలో నీళ్లు నిండిపోయినాయి. నాయన చాలా టెన్షన్ పడతాండాడు.
*   *Romanization:* (yēndi sāmi ī vānalu, asalu taggēṭaṭṭu lēvu. polamlō nīḷḷu niṇḍipōyināyi. nāyana cālā ṭenṣan paḍatāṇḍāḍu.)
*   *Standard Equivalent:* ఏమిటి దేవుడా ఈ వర్షాలు, అసలు తగ్గేటట్టు లేవు. పొలంలో నీళ్లు నిండిపోయాయి. నాన్న చాలా టెన్షన్ పడుతున్నాడు.
*   *Features visible:* 'yēndi sāmi' (what is this lord/man); 'niṇḍipōyināyi' (full past tense retention instead of niṇḍipōyāyi); 'nāyana' instead of 'nānna' (father); 'paḍatāṇḍāḍu' instead of 'paḍutunnāḍu' (is falling/worrying).

**3. Talking to an AI assistant (asking for help with something practical)**
*   *Native Script:* తిరుపతికి పోయే బస్సులు యెన్నింటికి ఉండాయి? నాకు రేపు పొద్దున్నే పోవాలా, టైమింగ్స్ చెప్పు.
*   *Romanization:* (tirupatiki pōyē bassulu yenniṇṭiki uṇḍāyi? nāku rēpu poddunnē pōvālā, ṭaimiṅgs ceppu.)
*   *Standard Equivalent:* తిరుపతికి వెళ్ళే బస్సులు ఎన్నింటికి ఉన్నాయి? నేను రేపు ఉదయాన్నే వెళ్ళాలి, టైమింగ్స్ చెప్పు.
*   *Features visible:* 'pōyē' instead of 'veḷḷē' (going); 'yenniṇṭiki' with 'y' glide; 'uṇḍāyi' instead of 'unnāyi' (are there); 'pōvālā' instead of 'veḷḷāli' (have to go).

---

### 6. What This Variety Does NOT Do

The most common error when generating Rayalaseema Telugu is confusing it with Telangana Telugu. While both are non-coastal dialects, they are distinct. Rayalaseema Telugu does **NOT** use Telangana past tense markers like -ఇండు (-iṇḍu) (e.g., చేసిండు / cēsiṇḍu). A Rayalaseema speaker says చేసినాడు (cēśināḍu). Rayalaseema does **NOT** use Telangana vocabulary like గతి (gati - condition) or పైలం (pailam - careful). 

Another common error is over-applying the 'y' glide. While *ekkaḍa* becomes *yāḍa*, not every word starting with a vowel gets a 'y'. For example, *amma* (mother) remains *amma* or becomes *avva*, it does not become *yamma*. Finally, a bad imitation often drops all respect markers, making the speaker sound like a movie villain. Authentic Rayalaseema speakers are highly respectful to elders and use *mīru* and *āyana* strictly; they do not use *nuvvu* (you) or *vāḍu* (he) for older individuals.

---

### 7. Persona Match Criteria

```yaml
persona_match:
  first_language: ["Telugu"]
  state: ["Andhra Pradesh"]
  district: ["Kadapa", "YSR Kadapa", "Kurnool", "Anantapur", "Chittoor", "Tirupati", "Nandyal", "Sri Sathya Sai", "Annamayya"]
  zone: ["Urban", "Rural", "Semi-urban"]
  education_level: ["High School", "Secondary", "Bachelor's", "College Dropout", "Vocational", "Primary"]
  occupation_keywords: ["farmer", "student", "driver", "shop owner", "mechanic", "teacher", "clerk", "business", "agriculture"]
  cultural_background_signals: ["Rayalaseema", "Seema", "faction", "groundnut", "drought-prone", "temple town", "Tirupati", "Rayalaseema slang"]
  linguistic_background_signals: ["Rayalaseema dialect", "Seema Telugu", "non-standard Telugu", "regional Telugu", "Telugu script"]
```

# Production Guide: Doabi (Punjabi)

## 1. Character and Feel

Doabi is one of the major dialects of Punjabi, spoken in the Doaba region of Punjab (the land between the Beas and Sutlej rivers), comprising the districts of Jalandhar, Hoshiarpur, Kapurthala, and Shaheed Bhagat Singh Nagar (Nawanshahr). Historically known as the NRI (Non-Resident Indian) hub of Punjab, the region's language is deeply intertwined with migration culture, prosperity, and a strong agrarian-meets-urban identity. In text, Doabi feels confident, direct, and heavily peppered with English loanwords related to travel, education, and foreign settlement. 

Phonologically, the most iconic and defining feature of Doabi—which surfaces prominently in informal chat spelling—is the substitution of the sound /v/ (ਵ) with /b/ (ਬ). A Doabi speaker will often type ਬਿੱਚ (bich) instead of ਵਿੱਚ (vich - in), ਬੱਡਾ (badda) instead of ਵੱਡਾ (vadda - big), and ਬੀ (bi) instead of ਵੀ (vi - also). Additionally, the Standard Punjabi present auxiliary ਹੈ (hai) is almost entirely replaced by ਆ (aa) or ਵਾ (wa), and the past auxiliary ਸੀ (si) is frequently expanded to ਸੀਗਾ/ਸੀਗੀ/ਸੀਗੇ (siga/sigi/sige). 

In digital communication (WhatsApp, Instagram, Snapchat), speakers use the Gurmukhi script or Roman Punjabi. For this guide, we focus on Gurmukhi script as used in chat. The spelling is highly phonetic, dropping silent letters and standard grammatical spaces. Diglossia exists between Standard written Punjabi (used in newspapers and formal education) and spoken Doabi, but in chat, the spoken Doabi register dominates completely. It is a prestige dialect within its own right, associated with the affluent NRI belt, and does not carry the "backward" stigma that some rural dialects face.

## 2. Grammar: Production Rules

### Verb Paradigm Tables

**1. Motion Verb: ਜਾਣਾ (To go)**

| Person | Standard | This Variety | Romanization |
|--------|----------|--------------|--------------|
| 1sg present | ਮੈਂ ਜਾਂਦਾ ਹਾਂ | ਮੈਂ ਜਾਂਦਾ ਆਂ / ਜਾਂਦਾ ਵਾਂ | main janda aan / janda waan |
| 2sg familiar present | ਤੂੰ ਜਾਂਦਾ ਹੈਂ | ਤੂੰ ਜਾਂਦਾ ਏਂ / ਜਾਂਦਾ ਵੇਂ | tu janda en / janda wen |
| 3sg present | ਉਹ ਜਾਂਦਾ ਹੈ | ਉਹ ਜਾਂਦਾ ਆ / ਜਾਂਦਾ ਵਾ | oh janda aa / janda wa |
| 1sg past | ਮੈਂ ਗਿਆ ਸੀ | ਮੈਂ ਗਿਆ ਸੀਗਾ | main gya siga |
| 3sg past | ਉਹ ਗਿਆ ਸੀ | ਉਹ ਗਿਆ ਸੀਗਾ | oh gya siga |
| 1sg future | ਮੈਂ ਜਾਵਾਂਗਾ | ਮੈਂ ਜਾਊਂਗਾ | main jaunga |
| 3sg future | ਉਹ ਜਾਵੇਗਾ | ਉਹ ਜਾਊਗਾ | oh jauga |

### Required Rule Categories

**1. Pronoun system differences**
Doabi speakers frequently contract the 2nd person respectful/plural pronouns. Standard ਤੁਹਾਡਾ (tuhada - your) becomes ਥੋਡਾ (thoda), and ਤੁਹਾਨੂੰ (tuhanu - to you) becomes ਥੋਨੂੰ (thonu). Third-person pronouns shift the initial vowel in spelling to match pronunciation: ਉਹ (uh) becomes ਓਹ (oh).

**2. Copula and auxiliary forms**
The Standard present auxiliary ਹੈ/ਹਨ (hai/han) is categorically replaced by ਆ/ਵਾ/ਆਂ (aa/wa/aan). The past auxiliary ਸੀ (si) is expanded with gender/number markers to ਸੀਗਾ/ਸੀਗੀ/ਸੀਗੇ (siga/sigi/sige).
*Affirmative Present Copula:*
- 1sg: ਆਂ / ਵਾਂ (aan / waan)
- 2sg fam: ਏਂ / ਵੇਂ (en / wen)
- 3sg: ਆ / ਵਾ (aa / wa)
- 1pl: ਆਂ (aan)
- 2pl/resp: ਓ (o)
- 3pl: ਆ / ਨੇ (aa / ne)
*Negative Present Copula:*
- 1sg: ਨੀਗਾ / ਨਹੀਂ ਆਂ (niga / nahi aan)
- 2sg fam: ਨੀਗਾ / ਨਹੀਂ ਏਂ (niga / nahi en)
- 3sg: ਨੀਗਾ / ਨੀਗੀ (niga / nigi)
- 1pl: ਨੀਗੇ (nige)
- 2pl/resp: ਨੀਗੇ (nige)
- 3pl: ਨੀਗੇ (nige)

**3. Past tense formation**
Punjabi does not have an ergative case marker like Hindi's 'ne' (ਨੇ) for pronouns in the same way, though it appears on nouns. In Doabi chat, the past tense is formed using the past participle followed by the expanded past auxiliary (ਸੀਗਾ/ਸੀਗੀ/ਸੀਗੇ). 
*Rule:* Subject + Object + Past Participle + ਸੀਗਾ/ਸੀਗੀ/ਸੀਗੇ.
*Example:* ਮੈਂ ਰੋਟੀ ਖਾਧੀ ਸੀਗੀ (I ate food).

**4. Perfect/completive tense**
To express "have done X", Doabi uses the past participle followed by the present Doabi copula (ਆ/ਆਂ).
*Pattern:* Past Participle + ਆ / ਆਂ.
*Examples:*
- ਮੈਂ ਕੰਮ ਕਰ ਲਿਆ ਆ (I have done the work).
- ਓਹ ਕੈਨੇਡਾ ਪਹੁੰਚ ਗਿਆ ਵਾ (He has reached Canada).
- ਅਸੀਂ ਫਾਈਲ ਲਾ ਦਿੱਤੀ ਆ (We have submitted the file).

**5. Future tense formation**
Standard future suffixes (-ਵਾਂਗਾ, -ਵੇਗਾ) are contracted. The 'v' sound is dropped or turns into a 'u' sound.
- 1sg: -ਊਂਗਾ / -ਊਂਗੀ (-unga / -ungi)
- 2sg: -ਏਂਗਾ / -ਏਂਗੀ (-enga / -engi)
- 3sg: -ਊਗਾ / -ਊਗੀ (-uga / -ugi)
- 1pl: -ਆਂਗੇ (-ange)
- 3pl: -ਣਗੇ (-nge)
*Example:* ਮੈਂ ਕੱਲ ਆਊਂਗਾ (I will come tomorrow).

**6. Compound verbs / auxiliary verb constructions**
Doabi heavily uses vector verbs to show completion, suddenness, or benefaction.
- ਲੈਣਾ (to take) for self-benefaction: ਮੈਂ ਟਿਕਟ ਕਰਵਾ ਲਈ ਆ (I got the ticket done for myself).
- ਦੇਣਾ (to give) for other-benefaction: ਓਹਨੂੰ ਪੈਸੇ ਭੇਜ ਤੇ (Sent the money to him - 'ਤੇ' is a contraction of ਦਿੱਤੇ).
- ਛੱਡਣਾ (to leave) for completion/force: ਮੈਂ ਕੰਮ ਕਰ ਛੱਡਿਆ (I finished off the work).
- ਪੈਣਾ (to fall) for continuous action: ਓਹ ਕੰਮ ਕਰਦਾ ਪਿਆ ਆ (He is currently doing the work).

**7. Negation**
The standard negation ਨਹੀਂ (nahin) is almost always shortened to ਨੀ (ni) in chat. It is placed immediately before the verb.
*Example:* ਮੈਂ ਨੀ ਜਾਣਾ ਓਥੇ (I am not going there).
For negative existence/copula, Doabi uses ਨੀਗਾ/ਨੀਗੀ (niga/nigi).
*Example:* ਮੇਰੇ ਕੋਲ ਪੈਸੇ ਨੀਗੇ (I don't have money).

**8. Question formation**
(a) Wh-questions: ਕੀ (what), ਕਿੱਦਾਂ (how), ਕਾਹਤੋਂ (why - replaces ਕਿਉਂ), ਕਿੱਥੇ (where), ਕਦੋਂ (when).
(b) Yes/no questions: There is no strict particle like Hindi's 'kya' at the start. Yes/no questions are marked purely by a question mark in text and context.
*Example:* ਤੂੰ ਆਉਣਾ ਆ? (Are you coming?)

**9. Sentence-final particles and discourse markers**
- ਯਾਰ (yaar): Used universally for camaraderie or mild frustration. (Standard: ਮਿੱਤਰ, but yaar is ubiquitous).
- ਭਾਜੀ / ਬਾਈ (bhaji / bai): Respectful address for an older brother or any male peer.
- ਨਾ (na): Used at the end to seek agreement. (e.g., ਤੂੰ ਜਾਊਗਾ ਨਾ? - You will go, right?).
- ਆਲਾ (aala): Suffix meaning 'the one with' (Standard: ਵਾਲਾ). e.g., ਬਾਹਰ ਆਲਾ (The one from abroad).

**10. Honorific/address system**
Two main levels:
- Familiar/Intimate: ਤੂੰ (tu) with 2sg verb agreements. Used for younger people, close friends, and often God.
- Respectful/Plural: ਤੁਸੀਂ (tusi) with 2pl verb agreements. Used for elders, strangers, and professionals. Using 'tu' for an elder is highly disrespectful.

## 3. Code-Mixing System

Doaba's massive NRI population means English code-mixing is not just for the educated elite; it is a fundamental part of the rural and urban vernacular. 

**Trigger conditions:** 
- *Domain triggers:* Anything related to immigration (visa, file, embassy, flight, PR, agent, IELTS, band, score), technology, and vehicles.
- *Formulaic triggers:* "ok", "sorry", "thanks", "wait", "try".

**Integration grammar:** 
English nouns take Punjabi postpositions seamlessly (e.g., ਫਲਾਈਟ 'ਚ - in the flight). English verbs are integrated using the Punjabi auxiliary ਕਰਨਾ (to do) or ਹੋਣਾ (to be) (e.g., apply ਕਰਤਾ - applied). Borrowed words are usually typed in Gurmukhi script phonetically, or occasionally in Roman script mixed into the Gurmukhi sentence.

**Examples at different mixing intensities:**

- **Low (One English word):**
  ਭਾਜੀ ਮੇਰਾ ਵੀਜ਼ਾ ਆ ਗਿਆ।
  (Bhaji mera visa aa gya.)
  *Brother, my visa has arrived.*

- **Medium (English noun/verb phrase):**
  ਮੈਂ ਏਜੰਟ ਨੂੰ ਫਾਈਲ ਸਬਮਿਟ ਕਰਤੀ ਆ।
  (Main agent nu file submit karti aa.)
  *I have submitted the file to the agent.*

- **High (English clause with native connective):**
  ਮੇਰੇ ਆਈਲੈਟਸ ਚੋਂ ਬੈਂਡ ਘੱਟ ਆਏ ਆ, so I think ਮੈਨੂੰ ਦੁਬਾਰਾ ਟਰਾਈ ਕਰਨਾ ਪੈਣਾ।
  (Mere IELTS chon band ghat aaye aa, so I think mainu dubara try karna paina.)
  *My IELTS bands came low, so I think I will have to try again.*

## 4. AI Interaction Pattern

A typical Doabi speaker interacts with an AI assistant in a highly transactional, practical manner, often treating it as a knowledgeable tool or a digital "agent." They are likely to ask for translations, travel information, or help drafting formal English messages. The tone is direct but not rude, often using polite markers like "please" or "ਭਾਜੀ" (brother) even with the AI. If the AI misunderstands, the speaker will likely switch to broken English or simplify their Punjabi, assuming the AI cannot handle their local dialect.

## 5. Three Authentic Chat Messages

**1. Casual/intimate (talking to a friend about meeting up)**
ਕਿੱਦਾਂ ਬੀਰੇ, ਕੀ ਕਰਦਾ ਪਿਆ ਆਂ? ਅੱਜ ਸ਼ਾਮ ਨੂੰ ਜਲੰਧਰ ਚੱਲੀਏ? ਮੇਰਾ ਥੋੜਾ ਜਿਹਾ ਕੰਮ ਸੀਗਾ ਓਥੇ। ਫਰੀ ਹੋਕੇ ਦੱਸੀਂ ਮੈਨੂੰ।
(Kiddan bire, ki karda pya aan? Ajj shaam nu Jalandhar challiye? Mera thoda jiha kamm siga othe. Free hoke dassin mainu.)
*Standard:* ਕਿਵੇਂ ਵੀਰ, ਕੀ ਕਰ ਰਹੇ ਹੋ? ਅੱਜ ਸ਼ਾਮ ਨੂੰ ਜਲੰਧਰ ਚੱਲੀਏ? ਮੇਰਾ ਥੋੜ੍ਹਾ ਜਿਹਾ ਕੰਮ ਸੀ ਉੱਥੇ। ਵਿਹਲੇ ਹੋ ਕੇ ਦੱਸਣਾ ਮੈਨੂੰ।
*Features visible:* 'ਬੀਰੇ' (bire - v to b shift from vire), 'ਕਰਦਾ ਪਿਆ ਆਂ' (continuous tense with pya + Doabi copula aan), 'ਸੀਗਾ' (Doabi past auxiliary).

**2. Slightly stressed/emotional (complaining about a visa delay)**
ਯਾਰ ਏਜੰਟ ਨੇ ਦਿਮਾਗ ਖਰਾਬ ਕਰਤਾ ਆ। ਦੋ ਮਹੀਨੇ ਹੋਗੇ ਫਾਈਲ ਲਾਈ ਨੂੰ, ਹਜੇ ਤੱਕ ਕੋਈ ਰਿਪਲਾਈ ਨੀ ਆਇਆ। ਬੱਡਾ ਭਰਾ ਬਣਕੇ ਪੁੱਛ ਕੇ ਦੱਸ ਓਹਨੂੰ ਕਾਹਤੋਂ ਲੇਟ ਆ।
(Yaar agent ne dimag kharab karta aa. Do mahine hoge file layi nu, haje tak koi reply ni aaya. Badda bhara banke puchh ke dass ohnu kahton late aa.)
*Standard:* ਯਾਰ ਏਜੰਟ ਨੇ ਦਿਮਾਗ ਖਰਾਬ ਕਰ ਦਿੱਤਾ ਹੈ। ਦੋ ਮਹੀਨੇ ਹੋ ਗਏ ਫਾਈਲ ਲਗਾਈ ਨੂੰ, ਹਾਲੇ ਤੱਕ ਕੋਈ ਜਵਾਬ ਨਹੀਂ ਆਇਆ। ਵੱਡਾ ਭਰਾ ਬਣ ਕੇ ਪੁੱਛ ਕੇ ਦੱਸ ਉਸਨੂੰ ਕਿਉਂ ਲੇਟ ਹੈ।
*Features visible:* 'ਕਰਤਾ ਆ' (contracted compound verb + Doabi copula), 'ਨੀ' (contracted negation), 'ਬੱਡਾ' (v to b shift from vadda), 'ਕਾਹਤੋਂ' (Doabi for why).

**3. Talking to an AI assistant (asking for translation help)**
ਭਾਜੀ ਮੇਰੀ ਇੱਕ ਹੈਲਪ ਕਰਿਓ। ਮੈਂ ਅੰਬੈਸੀ ਨੂੰ ਈਮੇਲ ਪਾਉਣੀ ਆ ਕਿ ਮੇਰਾ ਪਾਸਪੋਰਟ ਗੁੰਮ ਹੋ ਗਿਆ ਵਾ। ਇਹਨੂੰ ਇੰਗਲਿਸ਼ ਬਿੱਚ ਟਰਾਂਸਲੇਟ ਕਰ ਦੋ ਪਲੀਜ਼।
(Bhaji meri ik help kario. Main embassy nu email pauni aa ki mera passport gumm ho gya wa. Ehnu english bich translate kar do please.)
*Standard:* ਭਾਜੀ ਮੇਰੀ ਇੱਕ ਮਦਦ ਕਰਿਓ। ਮੈਂ ਅੰਬੈਸੀ ਨੂੰ ਈਮੇਲ ਪਾਉਣੀ ਹੈ ਕਿ ਮੇਰਾ ਪਾਸਪੋਰਟ ਗੁੰਮ ਹੋ ਗਿਆ ਹੈ। ਇਸਨੂੰ ਅੰਗਰੇਜ਼ੀ ਵਿੱਚ ਅਨੁਵਾਦ ਕਰ ਦਿਓ ਕਿਰਪਾ ਕਰਕੇ।
*Features visible:* 'ਪਾਉਣੀ ਆ' / 'ਗਿਆ ਵਾ' (Doabi present copulas aa/wa), 'ਬਿੱਚ' (v to b shift from vich), heavy English code-mixing (help, embassy, email, passport, translate, please).

## 6. What This Variety Does NOT Do

A common error when generating Doabi is confusing it with the Malwai dialect (spoken south of the Sutlej). Doabi does **not** use the Malwai past tense markers ਤੀ / ਤਾ / ਤੀਏ (ti / ta / tiye - e.g., Malwai "ਖਾਧੀ ਤੀ" vs Doabi "ਖਾਧੀ ਸੀਗੀ"). Doabi also does **not** use the Majhi continuous marker ਡਿਹਾ (diha - e.g., Majhi "ਕਰਨ ਡਿਹਾ" vs Doabi "ਕਰਦਾ ਪਿਆ"). 

Another major error is over-applying the v -> b shift. While it is highly characteristic of Doabi (ਵਿੱਚ -> ਬਿੱਚ, ਵੱਡਾ -> ਬੱਡਾ), it does not apply to every single word starting with 'v' in modern chat, especially English loanwords (a speaker will write 'visa', not 'bisa'). A bad imitation of Doabi sounds like a caricature of an uneducated villager, whereas real Doabi chat is highly modern, heavily mixed with English, and uses the v -> b shift as a natural phonological habit rather than a forced gimmick. Standard features that are *retained* include standard noun genders and standard pluralization rules, which should not be altered.

## 7. Persona Match Criteria

```yaml
persona_match:
  first_language: ["Punjabi", "Doabi", "Doabi Punjabi"]
  state: ["Punjab"]
  district: ["Jalandhar", "Hoshiarpur", "Kapurthala", "Shaheed Bhagat Singh Nagar", "Nawanshahr", "Phagwara"]
  zone: ["Urban", "Rural", "Semi-urban"]
  education_level: ["High School", "College", "Bachelor's", "Master's", "Vocational"]
  occupation_keywords: ["student", "farmer", "agent", "travel agent", "shopkeeper", "driver", "business", "NRI", "freelancer"]
  cultural_background_signals: ["Doaba", "NRI belt", "IELTS", "Canada migration", "UK migration", "agricultural", "Jalandhar city"]
  linguistic_background_signals: ["Doabi dialect", "uses b instead of v", "frequent English mixing", "informal Punjabi"]
```

# Production Guide: Kauravi (Rural Khariboli)

## 1. Character and Feel

Kauravi, often referred to as Rural Khariboli or simply "Dehati" by its speakers, is the prestige rural dialect of Western Uttar Pradesh (Meerut, Muzaffarnagar, Baghpat, Shamli, Saharanpur) and the rural fringes of Delhi. It is the direct ancestor of Standard Hindi but retains a distinct, robust agrarian character. In text, it sounds blunt, forceful, and highly informal. Outsiders often describe it as *lathmaar* (लठमार - sounding like a stick hitting the ground) due to its heavy prosody and directness, but within the community, it is a marker of brotherhood, agrarian pride, and authenticity.

Socially, Kauravi is strongly associated with the dominant agricultural communities of the region (Jats, Gujjars, Rajputs, and Tyagis). While educated speakers easily code-switch to Standard Hindi in formal settings, Kauravi dominates WhatsApp groups, Facebook comments, and intimate chats among family and friends. Urban youth from these regions often use it performatively online to assert a "desi" or "badmaash" (tough) identity. 

Phonologically, Kauravi's most striking feature in spelling is consonant gemination (doubling) to show emphasis or affection (e.g., *roti* becomes रोट्टी, *bapu* becomes बाप्पू). It heavily favors the retroflex nasal ण (ṇ) over the dental न (n) in medial and final positions (e.g., *pani* becomes पाणी). Vowels are often lowered or shortened compared to Standard Hindi (e.g., *paisa* becomes पेसा, *aur* becomes अर). In digital spaces, it is written in Devanagari or Hinglish, with writers intuitively modifying Standard Hindi spellings to reflect the heavy, drawled pronunciation of the region.

---

## 2. Grammar: Production Rules

### Verb Paradigm Tables

**1. Motion Verb: "to go" (जाना)**

| Person | Standard | This Variety | Romanization |
|--------|----------|--------------|--------------|

| 3sg present | जाता है | जावे है / जा रा | jāve hai / jā rā |
| 1sg past | गया | ग्या | gyā |
| 3sg past | गया | ग्या | gyā |
| 1sg future | जाऊँगा | जाऊँगा / जाउँग्गा | jāū̃gā / jāũggā |
| 3sg future | जाएगा | जावेगा / जाग्गा | jāvegā / jāggā |
| 1sg negative present | नहीं जाता हूँ | ना जाता / नी जा रा | nā jātā / nī jā rā |
| 3sg negative present | नहीं जाता है | ना जावे / नी जा रा | nā jāve / nī jā rā |
| 2sg imperative | जाओ | जा | jā |
| 2sg polite imperative | जाइए | चले जा / जाइयो | chale jā / jāiyo |

| 3sg present | होता है | होवे है / हो रा | hove hai / ho rā |
| 1sg past | हुआ | होया | hoyā |
| 3sg past | हुआ | होया | hoyā |

| 2sg imperative | हो जाओ | हो जा | ho jā |
| 2sg polite imperative | हो जाइए | हो जाइयो | ho jāiyo |

**3. Action Verb: "to do" (करना)**

| Person | Standard | This Variety | Romanization |
|--------|----------|--------------|--------------|
| 1sg present | करता हूँ | करूँ हूँ / कर रा | karū̃ hū̃ / kar rā |
| 2sg familiar present | करता है | करै है / कर रा | karai hai / kar rā |
| 3sg present | करता है | करै है / कर रा | karai hai / kar rā |
| 1sg past | किया | करा / करया | karā / karayā |
| 3sg past | किया | करा / करया | karā / karayā |
| 1sg future | करूँगा | करूँगा / करुँग्गा | karū̃gā / karũggā |
| 3sg future | करेगा | करैगा / करग्गा | karaigā / karaggā |
| 1sg negative present | नहीं करता हूँ | ना करता / नी कर रा | nā kartā / nī kar rā |
| 3sg negative present | नहीं करता है | ना करै / नी कर रा | nā karai / nī kar rā |
| 2sg imperative | करो | कर ले | kar le |
| 2sg polite imperative | कीजिए | कर लियो | kar liyo |

### Pronoun Table

| Person | Standard Nominative | Variety Nominative | Standard Dative/Accusative | Variety Dative/Accusative | Standard Genitive | Variety Genitive |
|--------|--------------------|--------------------|---------------------------|--------------------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1sg | मैं | मैं / मं | मुझे / मुझको | मन्ने / मेरे को | मेरा | मेरा / म्हारा |
| 2sg familiar | तू | तू | तुझे / तुझको | तन्ने / तेरे को | तेरा | तेरा / थारा |
| 2sg respectful | तुम | तम | तुम्हें / तुमको | तन्ने / थारे को | तुम्हारा | थारा / तुम्हारा |
| 2sg/3sg honorific | आप | आप / तम | आपको | आपको / थारे को | आपका | आपका / थारा |
| 3sg | यह / वह | यो / वो | इसे / उसे | इसने / उसने | इसका / उसका | इसका / उसका |
| 1pl | हम | हम | हमें / हमको | हमने / म्हारे को | हमारा | म्हारा |
| 3pl | ये / वे | ये / वे | इन्हें / उन्हें | इन्हने / उन्हने | इनका / उनका | इनका / उनका |

### Postpositions and Case Markers Table

| Function | Standard Form | Variety Form | Romanization | Example sentence in variety |
|----------|--------------|--------------|--------------|----------------------------|
| Dative (to) | को | ने / को | ne / ko | मन्ने बता दे। (Tell me.) |
| Genitive (of) | का / के / की | का / के / की | kā / ke / kī | यो छोरा रामू का है। (This boy is Ramu's.) |
| Locative (in/at) | में / पर | में / पै | mẽ / pai | खाट पै बैठ जा। (Sit on the cot.) |
| Instrumental (with/by) | से | ते / से | te / se | मेरे ते ना होवे यो काम। (This work won't be done by me.) |
| Ablative (from) | से | ते / से | te / se | दिल्ली ते कद आवेगा? (When will you come from Delhi?) |
| Purpose (for) | के लिए | खातर / के लिए | khātar / ke lie | तेरे खातर ल्याया हूँ। (I brought it for you.) |
| Comitative (with) | के साथ | गैल / के साथ | gail / ke sāth | मेरे गैल चाल। (Walk with me.) |

### Key Lexical Substitutions

| Category | Standard | Variety | Romanization | Usage note |
|----------|----------|---------|--------------|------------|
| Water | पानी | पाणी | pāṇī | Categorical shift of medial/final 'n' to 'ṇ'. |
| Good/fine | अच्छा / ठीक | बड़िया / चोखा | baṛiyā / chokhā | 'Chokha' is older/more rural; 'bariya' is very common. |
| Child (boy) | लड़का | छोरा / लौंडा | chhorā / lauṇḍā | 'Chhora' is standard Kauravi; 'launda' is slightly rougher. |
| Child (girl) | लड़की | छोरी | chhorī | Categorical replacement. |
| Come | आना | आणा | āṇā | Infinitive 'n' becomes 'ṇ'. |
| Go | जाना | जाणा | jāṇā | Infinitive 'n' becomes 'ṇ'. |
| What | क्या | के | ke | Categorical replacement in all contexts. |
| Why | क्यों | क्यूँ | kyū̃ | Pronounced with a heavier, longer nasalization. |
| Here | यहाँ | उरै / यहाँ | urai / yahā̃ | 'Urai' is deeply rural; 'yaha' is also used but shortened. |
| There | वहाँ | उड़ै / वहाँ | uṛai / vahā̃ | 'Urai/Urai' pair with 'Kade' (where). |
| Today | आज | आज | āj | Same as standard, but often emphasized. |
| Tomorrow | कल | काल | kāl | Vowel lengthening is common. |
| Now | अब | ईब | īb | Categorical replacement. |
| Home/house | घर | घर | ghar | Same, but often pronounced heavily. |
| Person/man | आदमी | माणस | māṇas | Used for a man or person in general. |
| Much/A lot | बहुत | घणा | ghaṇā | Categorical replacement. |
| Quickly | जल्दी | तावला / जल्दी | tāvlā / jaldī | "Tavla" means quick/hasty. |
| Where | कहाँ | कड़ै | kaṛai | Categorical replacement in deep rural speech. |
| Then | फिर | फेर | pher | Vowel lowering. |

### Required Rule Categories

**1. Pronoun system differences:**
Standard Hindi uses *mujhe/tujhe* for dative/accusative. Kauravi categorically uses *manne* (मन्ने) and *tanne* (तन्ने). These forms act as both Ergative (I did) and Dative (to me). Example: *Manne roti kha li* (I ate roti) and *Manne bata de* (Tell me). Plurals use *mhara* (म्हारा) instead of *hamara*.

**2. Copula and auxiliary forms:**
The present copula is often shortened or dropped. 
*   1sg: हूँ (hū̃)
*   2sg familiar: है (hai)
*   2sg respectful: हो (ho)
*   3sg: है (hai)
*   1pl: हैं (haĩ)
*   3pl: हैं (haĩ)
*Negative copula:* Standard uses *nahin hai*. Kauravi uses *na hai* (ना है) or simply *na* (ना) at the end of the sentence. Example: *Mera kaam na hai yo* (This is not my work).

**3. Past tense formation:**
Kauravi follows the ergative pattern like Standard Hindi, but the verb forms are often contracted. *Gaya* (went) becomes *gya* (ग्या). *Kiya* (did) becomes *kara* (करा). *Kaha* (said) becomes *kya* or *kaya* (क्या/कया).
*Rule:* Replace Standard -iya endings with -a or -ya.
*Standard:* उसने मुझे कहा (Usne mujhe kaha).
*Variety:* उसने मन्ने कया (Usne manne kaya).

**4. Perfect/completive tense:**
Formed using the past participle + shortened copula. The continuous/perfect boundary is often blurred with the use of *ra* (रा) instead of *raha* (रहा).
*Pattern:* Verb stem + लिया (liya) / दिया (diya).
*Examples:* 
1. मन्ने काम कर लिया। (I have done the work.)
2. वो ग्या। (He has gone - copula often dropped).
3. मैं आ रा हूँ। (I am coming / I have started coming).

**5. Future tense formation:**
Standard *-ega* / *-unga* endings are often geminated to *-agga* / *-ugga* or use the *-ve* infix.
*Pattern:* Verb stem + वेगा (vegā) or ग्गा (ggā).
*Examples:* वो जावेगा (He will go) or वो जाग्गा (He will go). मैं करूँगा (I will do).

**6. Compound verbs / auxiliary verb constructions:**
Verbs like *lena* (लेणा), *dena* (देणा), and *marna* (मारना) are heavily used as auxiliaries to show forceful completion.
*   *de* (दे): completion for others. *Bata de* (बता दे - tell).
*   *le* (ले): completion for self. *Kha le* (खा ले - eat).
*   *ger* (गेर - to drop/throw): used for sudden, forceful action. *Usne kaam kar gerya* (He forcefully/suddenly finished the work).

**7. Negation:**
Standard *nahin* (नहीं) is categorically replaced by *na* (ना) or *ni* (नी). It is placed immediately before the verb.
*Standard:* मैं नहीं जाऊँगा।
*Variety:* मैं ना जाउँग्गा / मैं नी जा रा।

**8. Question formation:**
*   (a) Wh-questions: *kya* -> के (ke), *kyon* -> क्यूँ (kyū̃), *kahan* -> कड़ै (kaṛai), *kaise* -> कुकर / कैसे (kukar / kaise).
*   (b) Yes/no questions: Indicated purely by intonation or by placing *ke* (के) at the end of the sentence. *Ke* has dual function (What / Yes-No marker).
*Example:* तू जा रा के? (Are you going?)

**9. Sentence-final particles and discourse markers:**
*   **रै (Rai):** Used to call out to someone of equal or lower status. *Rai chhore!* (Hey boy!). Standard: अरे (Are).
*   **भाई / भईया (Bhai / Bhaiya):** Used constantly as a filler or tag. *Na bhai* (No brother).
*   **बावलै (Bawlai):** Means "mad/crazy", used affectionately or in annoyance. *Ke kar ra bawlai?* (What are you doing, crazy?).

**10. Honorific/address system:**
Kauravi is notoriously egalitarian and informal. The 2nd person familiar *Tu* (तू) is used extensively, even for elders, parents, and God, without implying disrespect—it implies closeness. *Aap* (आप) is used only for distant strangers or in highly formal settings. Using *Aap* with a friend or family member sounds sarcastic or artificially distant.

---

## 3. Code-Mixing System

Code-mixing with English is highly productive but follows strict phonological adaptation rules. English is used primarily for modern technology, agriculture, and administration.

**Trigger conditions:** 
*   Domain triggers: Technology (फ़ोन/phone, नेट/net), Agriculture (टेक्टर/tractor, ट्यूबल/tube-well).
*   Formulaic triggers: "Time" (टेम), "Sorry" (सौरी), "Tension" (टेंसन).

**Integration grammar:** 
English nouns are fully integrated into native case structures. Native postpositions attach directly to English words. English verbs are rarely used as raw stems; they are converted into nouns and paired with native light verbs (करना/देना). Borrowed words are spelled in Devanagari to reflect rural pronunciation (e.g., Time -> टेम).

**Three examples at different mixing intensities:**

*   **Low (Single noun insertion):**
    *   *Variety:* भाई, ईब मेरे धोरै टेम ना है।
    *   *Romanization:* Bhāī, īb mere dhorai ṭem nā hai.
    *   *Gloss:* Brother, now me near time not is. (Brother, I don't have time right now.)
*   **Medium (English noun + native light verb):**
    *   *Variety:* मन्ने बापू को फून कर दिया था।
    *   *Romanization:* Manne bāppū ko phūn kar diyā thā.
    *   *Gloss:* I-erg father to phone do given was. (I had called father.)
*   **High (English phrase adapted to native phonology):**
    *   *Variety:* यो छोरा फुल टेंसन में है, इसका माइंड सैट नी हो रा।
    *   *Romanization:* Yo chhorā phul ṭeṃsan mẽ hai, iskā māiṇḍ saiṭ nī ho rā.
    *   *Gloss:* This boy full tension in is, his mind set not happening. (This boy is very stressed, his mind isn't settling down.)

---

## 4. AI Interaction Pattern

A typical Kauravi speaker treats an AI assistant as a practical, slightly magical tool, addressing it with direct, blunt commands rather than polite requests. There is no use of "please" or "kindly." The speaker expects the AI to understand their natural speech; if the AI misunderstands, the speaker will likely repeat the command using shorter, louder (in text, more emphatic) phrases, or switch to a slightly more Standard Hindi out of frustration, assuming the "machine" is too dumb to understand Dehati.

---

## 5. Three Authentic Chat Messages

**1. Casual/intimate (talking to a friend about meeting up)**
*   *Variety:* रै कड़ै है तू? ईब तक ना आया। जल्दी आ जा, हम उरै बाट देख रे तेरी।
*   *Romanization:* (Rai kaṛai hai tū? Īb tak nā āyā. Jaldī ā jā, ham urai bāṭ dekh re terī.)
*   *Standard:* अरे कहाँ है तू? अब तक नहीं आया। जल्दी आ जा, हम यहाँ तुम्हारा इंतज़ार कर रहे हैं।
*   *Features marked:* Use of *Rai* (discourse marker), *kaṛai* (where), *īb* (now), *urai* (here), *baat dekhna* (idiom for waiting).

**2. Slightly stressed/emotional (complaining about electricity for farming)**
*   *Variety:* भाई के बताऊँ, दो दिन ते बत्ती ना आ री। खेत में पाणी सूख ग्या सारा। दिमाग खराब हो रा कती।
*   *Romanization:* (Bhāī ke batāū̃, do din te battī nā ā rī. Khet mẽ pāṇī sūkh gyā sārā. Dimāg kharāb ho rā katī.)
*   *Standard:* भाई क्या बताऊँ, दो दिन से बिजली नहीं आ रही है। खेत में पानी सूख गया सारा। दिमाग बिल्कुल खराब हो रहा है।
*   *Features marked:* *ke* (what), *te* (from/since), *batti* (electricity), *paṇi* (water with retroflex), *kati* (absolutely/completely).

**3. Talking to an AI assistant (asking for weather/practical help)**
*   *Variety:* यो बता काल बारिश होवेगी के? मन्ने खेत में खाद घेरणा है। सही सही बताइये।
*   *Romanization:* (Yo batā kāl bāriś hovegī ke? Manne khet mẽ khād gherṇā hai. Sahī sahī batāiye.)
*   *Standard:* यह बताओ कल बारिश होगी क्या? मुझे खेत में खाद डालना है। सही सही बताना।
*   *Features marked:* *Yo bata* (blunt command), *kaal* (tomorrow), *hovegi ke* (future + yes/no marker *ke*), *manne* (to me/I), *gherna* (to throw/put).

---

## 6. What This Variety Does NOT Do

**Common errors when generating this variety:**
*   **Over-applying the retroflex 'ṇ' (ण):** While *pani* becomes *paṇi* and *apna* becomes *apṇa*, word-initial 'n' almost NEVER changes. *Naam* (name) remains *naam*, not *ṇaam*. *Nahi* remains *na/ni*, not *ṇi*. AI models often blindly replace every 'n' with 'ṇ', resulting in a cartoonish, unreadable caricature.
*   **Confusing it with Rajasthani or deep Haryanvi:** Kauravi does NOT use the Rajasthani copula *chhai* (छै). While it shares borders with Haryana, standard Kauravi uses *hai* (है) or *ho ra* (हो रा) rather than the deep Haryanvi *sai* (सै), though *sai* appears in border villages. Do not use *sai* categorically.
*   **Hyper-correction to Standard Hindi:** Models often wrongly drop the *manne/tanne* pronouns and revert to *mujhe/tujhe* because they look "more correct." A true Kauravi speaker will almost never type *mujhe* in an informal chat. 
*   **Politeness markers:** Adding *Aap* (आप) or *Kripya* (कृपया) instantly breaks the persona. Kauravi is built on the familiar *Tu* (तू) and imperative verbs.

---

## 7. Persona Match Criteria

```yaml
persona_match:
  first_language: ["Hindi", "Kauravi", "Khariboli", "Haryanvi"]
  state: ["Uttar Pradesh", "Delhi", "Haryana"]
  district: ["Meerut", "Muzaffarnagar", "Baghpat", "Shamli", "Saharanpur", "Bulandshahr", "Ghaziabad", "Hapur"]
  zone: ["Rural", "Semi-urban"]
  education_level: ["High School", "Intermediate", "Bachelor's", "Some College"]
  occupation_keywords: ["Farmer", "Agriculture", "Student", "Shopkeeper", "Driver", "Local business", "Dairy"]
  cultural_background_signals: ["Jat", "Gujjar", "Rajput", "Tyagi", "agrarian", "western UP", "desi", "village"]
  linguistic_background_signals: ["Dehati", "Khariboli", "Kauravi", "rough Hindi", "lathmaar", "rural Hindi"]
```
