# Production Guide: Ahirani (Khandeshi)

## 1. Character and Feel

Ahirani (also known as Khandeshi) is a vibrant Indo-Aryan language variety spoken primarily in the Khandesh region of North Maharashtra (Jalgaon, Dhule, Nandurbar districts) and adjacent parts of Nashik. While officially classified and administered as a dialect of Marathi, it possesses a distinct grammatical and lexical character heavily influenced by neighboring Gujarati and Bhili. In text, it sounds earthy, direct, and deeply rooted in the local agrarian and trading culture. It is a strong community marker; speaking or typing in Ahirani instantly establishes in-group solidarity and regional pride. 

In digital spaces like WhatsApp, Ahirani is the preferred register for casual, intimate, and humorous communication among family and friends. It lacks a standardized formal orthography, so speakers type it using the standard Marathi Devanagari keyboard, spelling words phonetically as they pronounce them. The phonological character is distinct: the Standard Marathi retroflex lateral 'ळ' (ḷ) often shifts to 'य' (y) or 'ल' (l) (e.g., *doḷā* becomes *doyā*), and the palatal sibilant 'श' (ś) is frequently pronounced and written as the dental 'स' (s). 

Grammatically, Ahirani diverges sharply from Standard Marathi in its case markers and copula. It uses *le* (ले) instead of *lā* (ला) for the dative, *mā* (मा) instead of *madhye* (मध्ये) for the locative, and *nā/nī/na* (ना/नी/न) instead of *cā/cī/ce* (चा/ची/चे) for the genitive. The most iconic marker of Ahirani is its present tense copula *śe* (शे), which replaces the Standard Marathi *āhe* (आहे). Code-mixing with Hindi and English is common among the youth, but the core Ahirani grammatical scaffolding (postpositions and copula) remains fiercely intact even when borrowing heavily.

---

## 2. Grammar: Production Rules

To convert Standard Marathi into Ahirani, apply the following transformation rules.

### Verb Paradigm Tables

**1. Motion Verb: "to go" (जाणे - jāṇe)**

| Person | Standard (Marathi) | This Variety (Ahirani) | Romanization |
|--------|--------------------|------------------------|--------------|
| 1sg present | जातो / जाते | जास | jās |
| 2sg familiar present | जातोस / जातेस | जास | jās |
| 3sg present | जातो / जाते | जास | jās |
| 1sg past | गेलो / गेले | गया / गई | gayā / gaī |
| 3sg past | गेला / गेली | गया / गई | gayā / gaī |
| 1sg future | जाईन | जासू / जाईन | jāsū / jāīn |
| 3sg future | जाईल | जाईन | jāīn |
| 1sg negative present | जात नाही | नही जात | nahī jāt |
| 3sg negative present | जात नाही | नही जात | nahī jāt |
| 2sg imperative | जा | जा | jā |
| 2sg polite imperative | जा | जाजा | jājā |

**2. Modal/State Verb: "to be" (असणे - asaṇe)**

| Person | Standard (Marathi) | This Variety (Ahirani) | Romanization |
|--------|--------------------|------------------------|--------------|
| 1sg present | आहे | शे | śe |
| 2sg familiar present | आहेस | शे | śe |
| 3sg present | आहे | शे | śe |
| 1sg past | होतो / होते | व्हता / व्हती | vhatā / vhatī |
| 3sg past | होता / होती | व्हता / व्हती | vhatā / vhatī |
| 1sg future | असेन | ऱ्हाईन | ṛhāīn |
| 3sg future | असेल | ऱ्हाईन | ṛhāīn |
| 1sg negative present | नाहीये / नाही | नहीये / नही | nahīye / nahī |
| 3sg negative present | नाहीये / नाही | नहीये / नही | nahīye / nahī |
| 2sg imperative | राहा | ऱ्हा | ṛhā |
| 2sg polite imperative | राहा | ऱ्हा | ṛhā |

**3. Action Verb: "to do" (करणे - karaṇe)**

| Person | Standard (Marathi) | This Variety (Ahirani) | Romanization |
|--------|--------------------|------------------------|--------------|
| 1sg present | करतो / करते | करस | karas |
| 2sg familiar present | करतोस / करतेस | करस | karas |
| 3sg present | करतो / करते | करस | karas |
| 1sg past | केले | करा | karā |
| 3sg past | केले | करा | karā |
| 1sg future | करेन | करसू / करीन | karasū / karīn |
| 3sg future | करेल | करीन | karīn |
| 1sg negative present | करत नाही | नही करत | nahī karat |
| 3sg negative present | करत नाही | नही करत | nahī karat |
| 2sg imperative | कर | कर | kar |
| 2sg polite imperative | करा | करा | karā |

### Pronoun Table

| Person | Standard Nominative | Variety Nominative | Standard Dative/Accusative | Variety Dative/Accusative | Standard Genitive | Variety Genitive |
|--------|--------------------|--------------------|---------------------------|--------------------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1sg | मी | मी | मला | माले / मले | माझा / माझी / माझे | मना / मनी / मन |
| 2sg familiar | तू | तू | तुला | तुले | तुझा / तुझी / तुझे | तुना / तुनी / तुन |
| 2sg respectful | तुम्ही | तुमी | तुम्हाला | तुमाले | तुमचा / तुमची / तुमचे | तुमना / तुमनी / तुमन |
| 2sg/3sg honorific | आपण | आपण | आपल्याला | आपणाले | आपला / आपली / आपले | आपना / आपनी / आपन |
| 3sg | तो / ती / ते | तो / ती / ते | त्याला / तिला | त्याले / तिले | त्याचा / तिचा | त्याना / तिना |
| 1pl | आम्ही | आमी | आम्हाला | आमाले | आमचा / आमची / आमचे | आमना / आमनी / आमन |
| 3pl | ते / त्या / ती | ते | त्यांना | त्यासले | त्यांचा / त्यांची / त्यांचे | त्यासना / त्यासनी / त्यासन |

### Postpositions and Case Markers Table

| Function | Standard Form | Variety Form | Romanization | Example sentence in variety |
|----------|--------------|--------------|--------------|----------------------------|
| Dative (to) | ला (lā) | ले (le) | le | रामले सांग. (Tell Ram.) |
| Genitive (of) | चा/ची/चे (cā/cī/ce) | ना/नी/न (nā/nī/na) | nā/nī/na | जळगावना रस्ता. (Road of Jalgaon.) |
| Locative (in/at) | आत/मध्ये (āt/madhye) | मा (mā) | mā | तो घरमा शे. (He is in the house.) |
| Instrumental (with/by) | ने (ne) | नी (nī) | nī | त्यानी करा. (He did it.) |
| Ablative (from) | कडून/हून (kaḍūn/hūn) | कडून / पायी (kaḍūn/pāyī) | kaḍūn / pāyī | मना कडून चुकल. (It was a mistake from me.) |
| Purpose (for) | साठी (sāṭhī) | करता (kartā) | kartā | तुना करता आना. (Brought it for you.) |
| Comitative (with) | सोबत/बरोबर (sobat/barobar) | संगे (saṅge) | saṅge | मना संगे चाल. (Walk with me.) |

### Key Lexical Substitutions

| Category | Standard | Variety | Romanization | Usage note |
|----------|----------|---------|--------------|------------|
| Boy/Son | मुलगा (mulgā) | पोऱ्या / अंडोर | poṛyā / aṇḍor | 'Aṇḍor' is very specific to rural Khandesh; 'poṛyā' is universal Ahirani. |
| Girl/Daughter | मुलगी (mulgī) | पोरी / अंडोरी | porī / aṇḍorī | Same as above. |
| Why | का (kā) | काबर | kābar | Categorical replacement in all contexts. |
| Here | इथे (ithe) | आठे | āṭhe | Categorical replacement. |
| There | तिथे (tithe) | तठे | taṭhe | Categorical replacement. |
| Where | कुठे (kuṭhe) | कठे | kaṭhe | Categorical replacement. |
| How much | किती (kitī) | कितला | kitlā | Used for quantity and time (e.g., "kitlā vāje" = what time). |
| Very/A lot | खूप (khūp) | भलत | bhalata | Used as an intensifier (e.g., "bhalata cāṅglā" = very good). |
| Good/Fine | छान (chān) | चोखट | chokhaṭ | Used for quality or appearance. |
| Father | वडील (vaḍīl) | बापू / आबा | bāpū / ābā | Common address and reference terms. |
| Mother | आई (āī) | माय | māy | Categorical replacement. |
| Quickly | लवकर (lavkar) | झटकन | jhaṭkan | Used for urging action. |
| Eyes | डोळे (doḷe) | डोये | doye | Demonstrates the ḷ -> y phonological shift. |
| Near | जवळ (javaḷ) | जवय | javay | Demonstrates the ḷ -> y phonological shift. |
| Tomorrow | उद्या (udyā) | सकाई | sakāī | Literally means "morning" but commonly used for "tomorrow" in Ahirani. |

### Required Rule Categories

1.  **Pronoun System:** Ahirani strictly replaces Standard Marathi genitive bases (*mājhā, tujhā, tyācā*) with *nā*-ending bases (*manā, tunā, tyānā*). Dative pronouns always take *le* (*māle, tule*). This is categorical and must be applied to every pronoun.
2.  **Copula and Auxiliary Forms:** The Standard Marathi *āhe* (is) is categorically replaced by *śe* (शे). 
    *   *Affirmative Paradigm:* 1sg: शे (śe), 2sg: शे (śe), 3sg: शे (śe), 1pl: शेत (śet), 3pl: शेत (śet).
    *   *Negative Paradigm:* 1sg: नहीये (nahīye), 2sg: नहीये (nahīye), 3sg: नहीये (nahīye), 1pl: नहीयेत (nahīyet), 3pl: नहीयेत (nahīyet).
3.  **Past Tense Formation:** Ahirani past tense verbs often end in *-ā* for masculine/general and *-ī* for feminine, dropping the Standard Marathi *-lo/-lā* endings. Example: *mī gelo* (I went) -> *mī gayā* (मी गया). *tyāne kele* (He did) -> *tyānī karā* (त्यानी करा).
4.  **Perfect/Completive Tense:** Formed using the past participle ending in *-el* (एल) followed by the copula *śe* (शे). 
    *   Pattern: Verb stem + *el* + *śe*.
    *   Examples: *karāyel śe* (करायेल शे - has done), *khāyel śe* (खायेल शे - has eaten), *gayel śe* (गयेल शे - has gone).
5.  **Future Tense Formation:** The 1st person future often takes the suffix *-sū* (सू) instead of Standard *-en*. Example: *mī jāsū* (मी जासू - I will go), *mī karasū* (मी करसू - I will do).
6.  **Compound Verbs:** Ahirani frequently uses auxiliary verbs to add nuance, similar to Marathi but with Ahirani forms.
    *   *ṭāk* (टाक - to put/drop): Marks completion. *karī ṭāk* (करी टाक - do it completely).
    *   *ghe* (घे - to take): Marks self-benefaction. *khāī ghe* (खाई घे - eat it up).
    *   *de* (दे - to give): Marks allowing/doing for others. *jāū de* (जाऊ दे - let it go).
7.  **Negation:** The negative marker is *nahī* (नही), placed *before* the main verb in present/future, unlike Standard Marathi which often places it after. Example: *mī nahī jāt* (मी नही जात - I am not going). For imperatives, *nako* (नको) is used: *karū nako* (करू नको - don't do).
8.  **Question Formation:** 
    *   *Wh-questions:* Use Ahirani specific interrogatives: *kābar* (why), *kaṭhe* (where), *kitlā* (how much).
    *   *Yes/No questions:* Tone-based, or marked by adding *kāy* (काय) at the end of the sentence. Example: *tū jās kāy?* (तू जास काय? - Are you going?).
9.  **Discourse Markers:**
    *   **भो (bho):** The most iconic Ahirani marker. Means "bro/dude/man". Used constantly for emphasis and camaraderie. *kāy bho!* (What's up, man!).
    *   **वं (vaṃ):** Used when addressing women respectfully or affectionately (similar to 'go' in Marathi).
    *   **रे (re):** Used for younger males or close friends.
10. **Honorific/Address System:** Two levels. Familiar/intimate uses *tū* (तू) and singular verbs. Respectful/plural uses *tumī* (तुमी) and plural verbs (*śet* instead of *śe*). Using *tū* with elders is considered highly disrespectful, though less rigidly enforced than in Puneri Marathi.

---

## 3. Code-Mixing System

Ahirani speakers, especially youth and urban residents, freely mix English and Hindi into their digital communication. 

**Trigger conditions:**
*   **Domain triggers:** Technology, education, and modern administration (e.g., *mobile, office, train, tension, problem*).
*   **Formulaic triggers:** Greetings and apologies (*Good morning, sorry, thanks, okay*).

**Integration grammar:**
English nouns are seamlessly integrated into Ahirani syntax using Ahirani postpositions. The postpositions attach directly to the English word, often typed in Devanagari or Roman. English verbs are usually borrowed as nouns and paired with the Ahirani verb *kar* (to do) or *ho* (to happen).

**Examples at different mixing intensities:**

*   **Low (Single word insertion):**
    *   *Native script:* आज भलत tension शे भो.
    *   *Romanization:* āj bhalata tension śe bho.
    *   *Gloss:* Today very tension is bro. (I am very stressed today, bro.)
*   **Medium (English noun with Ahirani case marker):**
    *   *Native script:* मी office-मा शे, नंतर call करस.
    *   *Romanization:* mī office-mā śe, naṃtar call karas.
    *   *Gloss:* I office-in am, later call do. (I am in the office, I'll call later.)
*   **High (English clause with native connective):**
    *   *Native script:* I will let you know, पण माले टाईम लागीन.
    *   *Romanization:* I will let you know, paṇ māle ṭāīm lāgīn.
    *   *Gloss:* I will let you know, but to-me time will-attach. (I will let you know, but it will take me some time.)

---

## 4. AI Interaction Pattern

An Ahirani speaker interacting with an AI assistant views it as a practical tool. They are likely to approach it with direct, functional requests rather than overly polite phrasing. While they might initially default to Standard Marathi or Hindi assuming the AI won't understand Ahirani, if they realize the AI *can* process Ahirani, they will use it enthusiastically, mixing in English tech terms. Error recovery usually involves switching to Hindi or Standard Marathi, assuming the AI's "local language" module failed.

---

## 5. Three Authentic Chat Messages

**1. Casual/intimate (Texting a friend to meet up)**
*   *Native script:* काय भो, कठे शे तू? लवकर ये घरमा. आपण बाहेर जासू.
*   *Romanization:* (kāy bho, kaṭhe śe tū? lavkar ye gharmā. āpaṇ bāher jāsū.)
*   *Standard equivalent:* काय रे, कुठे आहेस तू? लवकर ये घरात. आपण बाहेर जाऊ.
*   *Features marked:* Use of discourse marker *bho* (भो); Ahirani interrogative *kaṭhe* (कठे); Ahirani copula *śe* (शे); Locative postposition *mā* (मा); Future tense suffix *sū* (सू).

**2. Slightly stressed/emotional (Complaining about work/weather)**
*   *Native script:* आज भलत काम शे यार, कंटाळा येई गया माले. सकाई सुट्टी घेसू मी.
*   *Romanization:* (āj bhalata kām śe yār, kaṇṭāḷā yeī gayā māle. sakāī suṭṭī ghesū mī.)
*   *Standard equivalent:* आज खूप काम आहे यार, कंटाळा आला मला. उद्या सुट्टी घेईन मी.
*   *Features marked:* Intensifier *bhalata* (भलत); Copula *śe* (शे); Compound past verb *yeī gayā* (येई गया); Dative pronoun *māle* (माले); Lexical substitution *sakāī* (सकाई) for tomorrow.

**3. Talking to an AI assistant (Asking for practical help)**
*   *Native script:* जळगाव ची ट्रेन कितला वाजे शे? माले तिकीट बुक करायेल शे.
*   *Romanization:* (jaḷgāv cī ṭren kitlā vāje śe? māle tikīṭ buk karāyel śe.)
*   *Standard equivalent:* जळगाव ची ट्रेन किती वाजता आहे? मला तिकीट बुक करायचे आहे.
*   *Features marked:* Ahirani interrogative *kitlā* (कितला); Copula *śe* (शे); Dative pronoun *māle* (माले); Perfective/Completive construction *karāyel śe* (करायेल शे). *(Note: 'cī' is retained here as a common borrowing from Standard Marathi in formal/travel contexts, though 'nī' could also be used).*

---

## 6. What This Variety Does NOT Do

A common error when generating Ahirani is treating it merely as Standard Marathi with a few misspelled words. Ahirani is grammatically distinct. 
*   **Do NOT use *lā* (ला) or *cā/cī/ce* (चा/ची/चे).** A true Ahirani sentence almost never uses the Standard Marathi dative or genitive markers. If you write "मला" (malā) instead of "माले" (māle), the illusion of Ahirani is instantly broken.
*   **Do NOT drop the copula *śe* (शे).** Some models try to create a "rural" feel by just dropping the verb "to be" entirely. Ahirani relies heavily on *śe*. 
*   **Do NOT over-apply obscure rural vocabulary.** Words like *aṇḍor* (boy) are authentic but highly specific to deep rural pockets. Overusing them in a modern WhatsApp chat makes the text read like a dialect parody or a folk play. Stick to high-frequency grammatical markers (*mā, le, nā, śe*) and common words (*bho, kaṭhe, kābar*) for an authentic digital register.
*   **Do NOT use Puneri Marathi discourse markers.** Avoid words like *mhaṇje*, *tar*, or *kī* in their strict Standard Marathi sense; Ahirani uses its own flow and rhythm.

---

## 7. Persona Match Criteria

```yaml
persona_match:
  first_language: ["Marathi", "Ahirani", "Khandeshi", "Bhili"]
  state: ["Maharashtra"]
  district: ["Jalgaon", "Dhule", "Nandurbar", "Nashik"]
  zone: ["Urban", "Semi-urban", "Rural"]
  education_level: ["High School", "Some College", "Bachelors", "Masters"]
  occupation_keywords: ["farmer", "student", "shopkeeper", "trader", "clerk", "business", "agriculture", "teacher"]
  cultural_background_signals: ["Khandesh", "North Maharashtra", "agri", "farming community", "cotton", "banana farming"]
  linguistic_background_signals: ["speaks Ahirani at home", "Khandeshi dialect", "mixes Marathi and Ahirani", "rural Marathi"]
```

# Production Guide: Puadhi (Powadhi) Variety of Punjabi

## 1. Character and Feel

Puadhi (Powadhi) is an eastern dialect of Punjabi spoken in the Powadh region, which serves as a linguistic bridge between the Punjabi-speaking heartland (Majha/Malwa) and the Hindi/Haryanvi-speaking belt to the south and east. Spoken primarily in Rupnagar (Ropar), SAS Nagar (Mohali), eastern Patiala, Fatehgarh Sahib, and the adjoining areas of Haryana (Ambala, Panchkula), it is characterized by a fascinating blend of Punjabi vocabulary and syntax with distinct morphological features that echo neighboring Indo-Aryan languages like Haryanvi and Braj. 

In text, Puadhi sounds earthy, direct, and deeply rooted in the local community. It lacks the urban prestige of Standard Majhi or the dominant pop-culture presence of Malwai, often being perceived as a "rural" or "rustic" community marker. However, among urban youth in Mohali and Chandigarh peripheries, Puadhi features are increasingly reclaimed as a marker of authentic local identity. A reader will immediately recognize Puadhi through its distinct pronouns (using ਹਮ/ਥਮ instead of ਅਸੀਂ/ਤੁਸੀਂ) and its signature past tense copula (ਥਾ/ਥੀ/ਥੇ instead of Standard Punjabi ਸੀ). 

Phonologically, Puadhi often substitutes the Standard Punjabi /v/ (ਵ) with /m/ (ਮ) or /b/ (ਬ) in certain environments, which surfaces in spelling (e.g., ਤੀਵੀਂ becomes ਤੀਮੀਂ, and future tense ਜਾਵਾਂਗਾ becomes ਜਾਮਾਂਗਾ). Diglossia is highly prevalent: most educated Puadhi speakers write in Standard Punjabi or English for formal communication, reserving Puadhi for intimate WhatsApp chats, voice notes, and informal social media interactions. When typed in Gurmukhi, speakers often use phonetic spellings to capture their exact pronunciation, deliberately deviating from standard orthography to signal in-group solidarity.

---

## 2. Grammar: Production Rules

### Verb Paradigm Tables

**1. Motion Verb: ਜਾਣਾ (to go)**

| Person | Standard | This Variety | Romanization |
|--------|----------|--------------|--------------|
| 1sg present | ਜਾਂਦਾ ਹਾਂ | ਜਾਂਦਾ ਹਾਂ / ਜਾਂਦਾ ਆਂ | jāndā hā̃ / jāndā ā̃ |
| 2sg familiar present | ਜਾਂਦਾ ਹੈਂ | ਜਾਂਦਾ ਐ | jāndā ai |

### Required Rule Categories

**1. Pronoun system differences from Standard**
Puadhi radically departs from Standard Punjabi in its 1st and 2nd person plural pronouns. Convert Standard ਅਸੀਂ (we) to ਹਮ, and ਤੁਸੀਂ (you pl.) to ਥਮ or ਥਾਮੇ. Consequently, the genitive forms shift: ਸਾਡਾ (our) becomes ਮ੍ਹਾਰਾ, and ਤੁਹਾਡਾ (your) becomes ਥਾਰਾ. 
*Rule:* Standard `ਅਸੀਂ ਜਾਵਾਂਗੇ` -> Puadhi `ਹਮ ਜਾਮਾਂਗੇ`.

**2. Copula and auxiliary forms (MANDATORY)**
The most defining feature of Puadhi is its past tense copula, which aligns with Hindi/Haryanvi rather than Standard Punjabi. Standard Punjabi uses ਸੀ (sī) for all persons/genders in the past. Puadhi uses ਥਾ/ਥੀ/ਥੇ/ਥੀਆਂ (thā/thī/thē/thīā̃) which inflects for gender and number.
*Present Affirmative:* 1sg: ਆਂ/ਹਾਂ, 2sg: ਐ/ਹੈਂ, 3sg: ਐ/ਹੈ, 1pl: ਆਂ/ਹਾਂ, 3pl: ਨੇ/ਹਨ.
*Present Negative:* 1sg: ਨੀ ਆਂ, 2sg: ਨੀ ਐ, 3sg: ਨੀ ਐ, 1pl: ਨੀ ਆਂ, 3pl: ਨੀ ਨੇ.
*Past Affirmative:* 1sg(m): ਥਾ, 1sg(f): ਥੀ, 2sg(m): ਥਾ, 2sg(f): ਥੀ, 3sg(m): ਥਾ, 3sg(f): ਥੀ, 1pl: ਥੇ, 3pl(m): ਥੇ, 3pl(f): ਥੀਆਂ.
*Past Negative:* 1sg(m): ਨੀ ਥਾ, 1sg(f): ਨੀ ਥੀ, 3sg(m): ਨੀ ਥਾ, 3pl(m): ਨੀ ਥੇ.
*Rule:* Standard `ਉਹ ਜਾਂਦਾ ਸੀ` -> Puadhi `ਉਹ ਜਾਂਦਾ ਥਾ`.

**3. Past tense formation**
While Puadhi uses many of the same irregular past participles as Standard Punjabi (e.g., ਗਿਆ for went), it often regularizes verbs ending in consonants by adding -ਇਆ (-iā) where Standard might use an irregular form. 
*Rule:* Standard `ਕੀਤਾ` (did) -> Puadhi `ਕਰਿਆ` (karia). Standard `ਦਿੱਤਾ` (gave) -> Puadhi `ਦਿਆ` (dia).

**4. Perfect/completive tense**
Formed using the past participle followed by the present copula. 
*Pattern:* Verb(Past Participle) + Present Copula (ਐ/ਆਂ/ਨੇ).
*Examples:* 
1. ਮੈਂ ਰੋਟੀ ਖਾ ਲਈ ਐ। (I have eaten food.)
2. ਉਹ ਘਰ ਆ ਗਿਆ ਐ। (He has come home.)
3. ਹਮਨੇ ਕੰਮ ਕਰਿਆ ਐ। (We have done the work.)

**5. Future tense formation**
Puadhi features a distinct /m/ infix in the 1st person future tense, replacing the Standard /v/. 
*Pattern:* 1sg: -ਮਾਂਗਾ (-mā̃gā), 1pl: -ਮਾਂਗੇ (-mā̃gē). 
*Rule:* Standard `ਮੈਂ ਖਾਵਾਂਗਾ` -> Puadhi `ਮੈਂ ਖਾਮਾਂਗਾ`. Standard `ਅਸੀਂ ਆਵਾਂਗੇ` -> Puadhi `ਹਮ ਆਮਾਂਗੇ`.

**6. Compound verbs / auxiliary verb constructions**
Like Standard Punjabi, Puadhi uses vector verbs to add nuance (completion, suddenness, benefaction).
* ਲੈਣਾ (to take - self-benefactive): ਕਰ ਲੈ (do it for yourself).
* ਦੇਣਾ (to give - other-benefactive): ਦੱਸ ਦੇ (tell them).
* ਛੱਡਣਾ / ਮਾਰਨਾ (completion/force): ਖਾ ਛੱਡਿਆ ਥਾ (had eaten it completely).

**7. Negation**
Negation is marked by ਨੀ (nī) or ਨਹੀਂ (nahī̃) placed immediately before the verb or auxiliary. In imperative sentences, ਨਾ (nā) is used.
*Rule:* Standard `ਮੈਂ ਨਹੀਂ ਜਾਣਾ` -> Puadhi `ਮੈਂ ਨੀ ਜਾਣਾ`. Standard `ਇਹ ਨਾ ਕਰ` -> Puadhi `ਇਹ ਨਾ ਕਰ`.

**8. Question formation**
(a) Wh-questions: Use Puadhi specific interrogatives: ਕਿਆ (what), ਕਦ (when), ਕਾਹਤੇ (why). 
(b) Yes/No questions: Indicated purely by rising intonation in speech, and a question mark in text. No specific particle is added at the beginning (unlike Hindi 'kya'). 
*Rule:* Standard `ਤੂੰ ਕੀ ਕਰਦਾ ਹੈਂ?` -> Puadhi `ਤੂੰ ਕਿਆ ਕਰਦਾ ਐ?`

**9. Sentence-final particles and discourse markers**
* ਬਾਈ / ਭਾਈ (bāī / bhāī): Used extensively as a friendly address term (bro/mate).
* ਯਾਰ (yār): Universal informal marker of camaraderie or frustration.
* ਤਾ (tā): Emphatic particle (Standard: ਤਾਂ). Example: `ਮੈਂ ਤਾ ਨੀ ਜਾਣਾ` (I am definitely not going).

**10. Honorific/address system**
Puadhi is slightly less rigidly hierarchical than Majhi. The plural is used for respect (e.g., using ਥਮ for an elder instead of ਤੂੰ). Using ਤੂੰ with elders is considered rude, but among peers, it is the default.

---

## 3. Code-Mixing System

Puadhi speakers, especially in urbanizing areas like Mohali and Zirakpur, heavily code-mix with English. 

**Trigger conditions:** 
*   **Domain triggers:** Technology, education, and modern administration (e.g., "class", "phone", "time", "message").
*   **Formulaic triggers:** "Okay", "sorry", "thanks", "wait".

**Integration grammar:** 
English nouns take Punjabi postpositions (e.g., ਕਲਾਸ ਬਿੱਚ - in class). English verbs are integrated using the native auxiliary ਕਰਨਾ (to do) or ਹੋਣਾ (to be), which then take Puadhi inflections (e.g., ਸੈਂਡ ਕਰਿਆ ਥਾ - had sent). Borrowed words are usually typed in Gurmukhi script in these chats, adapted to local phonology (e.g., ਟੈਮ for time).

**Examples at different mixing intensities:**

*   **Low (Single noun insertion):**
    *   Native script: ਮੇਰੇ ਧੋਰੇ ਟੈਮ ਨੀ ਐ ਬਾਈ।
    *   Romanization: mērē dhōrē ṭaim nī ai bāī.
    *   Gloss: me-near time not is bro. (I don't have time, bro.)
*   **Medium (English verb + native auxiliary):**
    *   Native script: ਮੈਂ ਉਹਨੂੰ ਮੈਸੇਜ ਸੈਂਡ ਕਰਿਆ ਥਾ।
    *   Romanization: maĩ uhnū̃ maisēj saiṇḍ kariā thā.
    *   Gloss: I him message send did was. (I had sent him a message.)
*   **High (English clause with native connective):**
    *   Native script: ਮੈਂ ਆਊਂਗਾ but ਯਾਰ ਇਬ ਮੈਂ busy ਆਂ।
    *   Romanization: maĩ āū̃gā but yār ib maĩ busy ā̃.
    *   Gloss: I will-come but man now I busy am. (I will come but man, I am busy right now.)

---

## 4. AI Interaction Pattern

A typical Puadhi speaker interacting with an AI assistant views it as a practical tool rather than a human authority figure. Because AI interfaces historically default to Standard Hindi or English, a speaker might initially code-switch to Standard Punjabi or Hindi, assuming the AI won't understand their local dialect. If they do use Puadhi (e.g., via voice typing or casual text), they will use direct commands ("ਇਹ ਦੱਸ" - tell me this) rather than overly polite requests. If the AI misunderstands, the user is highly likely to abandon the dialect and switch to English keywords or Standard Hindi to get the job done.

---

## 5. Three Authentic Chat Messages

**1. Casual/intimate (talking to a friend)**
*   **Native script:** ਬਾਈ ਥਮ ਕਦ ਆਓਗੇ? ਮੈਂ ਤਾ ਕਦ ਦਾ ਉਰੇ ਬੈਠਾ ਆਂ। ਮ੍ਹਾਰੇ ਗੈਲ ਚੱਲਿਓ ਅੱਜ।
*   **Romanization:** (bāī tham kad āōgē? maĩ tā kad dā urē baiṭhā ā̃. mhārē gail calliō ajj.)
*   **Standard equivalent:** ਵੀਰੇ ਤੁਸੀਂ ਕਦੋਂ ਆਓਗੇ? ਮੈਂ ਤਾਂ ਕਦੋਂ ਦਾ ਇੱਥੇ ਬੈਠਾ ਹਾਂ। ਸਾਡੇ ਨਾਲ ਚੱਲਿਓ ਅੱਜ।
*   **Features visible:** Use of ਥਮ (you), ਕਦ (when), ਉਰੇ (here), ਮ੍ਹਾਰੇ (our), ਗੈਲ (with).

**2. Slightly stressed/emotional (complaining)**
*   **Native script:** ਯਾਰ ਕਿਆ ਦੱਸਾਂ, ਕੱਲ੍ਹ ਮੈਂ ਕੰਮ ਤੇ ਲੇਟ ਹੋ ਗਿਆ ਥਾ। ਬੌਸ ਨੇ ਕਸੂਤਾ ਦਿਮਾਗ ਖਰਾਬ ਕਰਿਆ। ਇਬ ਮੇਰੇ ਧੋਰੇ ਪੈਸੇ ਵੀ ਨੀ।
*   **Romanization:** (yār kiā dassā̃, kallh maĩ kamm tē lēṭ hō giā thā. baus nē kasūtā dimāg kharāb kariā. ib mērē dhōrē paisē vī nī.)
*   **Standard equivalent:** ਯਾਰ ਕੀ ਦੱਸਾਂ, ਕੱਲ੍ਹ ਮੈਂ ਕੰਮ ਤੋਂ ਲੇਟ ਹੋ ਗਿਆ ਸੀ। ਬੌਸ ਨੇ ਬਹੁਤ ਦਿਮਾਗ ਖਰਾਬ ਕੀਤਾ। ਹੁਣ ਮੇਰੇ ਕੋਲ ਪੈਸੇ ਵੀ ਨਹੀਂ।
*   **Features visible:** Use of ਕਿਆ (what), ਥਾ (past copula), ਕਰਿਆ (past tense of do), ਇਬ (now), ਧੋਰੇ (near/have).

**3. Talking to an AI assistant (practical request)**
*   **Native script:** ਮੋਹਾਲੀ ਬਿੱਚ ਅੱਜ ਮੌਸਮ ਕੈਸਾ ਰਹੂਗਾ? ਕਿਆ ਮੀਂਹ ਪੈਣ ਦਾ ਚਾਂਸ ਐ?
*   **Romanization:** (mōhālī bicc ajj mausam kaisā rahūgā? kiā mī̃h paiṇ dā cāns ai?)
*   **Standard equivalent:** ਮੋਹਾਲੀ ਵਿੱਚ ਅੱਜ ਮੌਸਮ ਕਿਹੋ ਜਿਹਾ ਰਹੇਗਾ? ਕੀ ਮੀਂਹ ਪੈਣ ਦਾ ਚਾਂਸ ਹੈ?
*   **Features visible:** Use of ਬਿੱਚ (in - /v/ to /b/ shift), ਕੈਸਾ (how - Hindi influence common in Puadhi), ਕਿਆ (what/question marker).

---

## 6. What This Variety Does NOT Do

A common error when generating Puadhi is treating it exactly like Standard Haryanvi or Hindi just because it shares some vocabulary (like ਥਾ/ਥੀ). Puadhi is fundamentally a **Punjabi** dialect. It retains Punjabi verb roots (ਖਾ, ਪੀ, ਜਾ, ਆ), Punjabi noun genders, and Punjabi sentence structures. For example, a bad imitation might say "ਮੈਂ ਜਾਤਾ ਥਾ" (I used to go - using Hindi 'jata'), whereas authentic Puadhi says "ਮੈਂ ਜਾਂਦਾ ਥਾ" (using Punjabi present participle 'janda' + Puadhi past copula 'tha').

Another common mistake is over-applying the /m/ substitution. While 'ਜਾਵਾਂਗਾ' becomes 'ਜਾਮਾਂਗਾ', not every 'v' becomes 'm'. Words like 'ਵਿੱਚ' (in) usually become 'ਬਿੱਚ' (bicc), not 'ਮਿੱਚ'. Furthermore, AI models often wrongly drop the Puadhi pronouns (ਹਮ, ਥਮ) and revert to Standard Punjabi (ਅਸੀਂ, ਤੁਸੀਂ) while keeping the past tense 'ਥਾ'. An authentic Puadhi sentence must maintain internal consistency: if the speaker is using 'ਥਾ', they are highly likely to use 'ਹਮ/ਥਮ' and 'ਗੈਲ' as well.

---

## 7. Persona Match Criteria

  first_language: ["Punjabi", "Puadhi", "Powadhi", "Hindi"]
  state: ["Punjab", "Haryana", "Chandigarh"]

# Production Guide: Hyderabadi Urban Telugu

## 1. Character and Feel

Hyderabadi Urban Telugu is the vibrant, fast-paced, and highly syncretic variety of Telugu spoken in the Hyderabad and Secunderabad metropolitan area. It is characterized by a massive influx of Dakhini Urdu and English vocabulary layered over a Telangana Telugu grammatical substrate. In text and chat, it sounds punchy, informal, and highly expressive. It is the language of urban youth, IT professionals, and everyday street interactions, carrying a strong local identity ("Hyderabadi") that distances itself from the textbook-standard Coastal Andhra Telugu.

Phonologically, this variety is marked by vowel shortening, the dropping of final vowels in rapid speech (often reflected in casual typing), and the de-aspiration of loanwords. The retroflex consonants are pronounced strongly, but the complex consonant clusters of Standard Telugu are often simplified. In spelling, speakers using the Telugu script will often write words exactly as they sound in the local dialect (e.g., writing చేస్తాండు instead of the standard చేస్తున్నాడు). 

Grammatically, Hyderabadi Telugu features distinct verb conjugations, particularly in the present continuous and past tenses. The 3rd person masculine singular ending heavily favors *-ṇḍu* (ండు) over the standard *-ḍu* (డు). Postpositions also differ significantly, with *la* (ల) replacing *lō* (లో) for "in", and *tōni* (తోని) replacing *tō* (తో) for "with". 

Code-mixing is not just an occasional feature; it is the defining characteristic of this variety. A typical sentence seamlessly weaves Telugu grammar with Dakhini Urdu adjectives and English nouns. Diglossia is extremely high: a speaker will use this variety exclusively for WhatsApp, social media, and verbal communication, but would switch to Standard Telugu for formal writing, reading the news, or academic contexts.

---

## 2. Grammar: Production Rules

### Verb Paradigm Tables

### Key Lexical Substitutions

|----------|----------|---------|--------------|------------|

| Child (boy) | అబ్బాయి | పోరడు / పోరగాడు | pōraḍu / pōragāḍu | Very common in Hyderabad for "boy/guy". |
| Child (girl) | అమ్మాయి | పోరి | pōri | Very common for "girl/young woman". |
| Here | ఇక్కడ | ఈడ | īḍa | Categorical replacement in speech/chat. |
| There | అక్కడ | ఆడ | āḍa | Categorical replacement in speech/chat. |
| Where | ఎక్కడ | ఏడ | ēḍa | Categorical replacement in speech/chat. |
| Inside | లోపల | లోపట | lōpaṭa | Phonological shift typical of Telangana. |
| Good/fine | బాగుంది | మస్తు ఉంది / కిరాక్ | mastu undi / kirāk | 'Mastu' (Dakhini) means 'a lot/great'. 'Kirak' means awesome/crazy good. |
| Anger/Crazy | కోపం / పిచ్చి | దిమాక్ ఖరాబ్ | dimāk kharāb | Dakhini phrase used categorically for frustration. |
| Quickly | త్వరగా | జల్దీ | jaldī | Dakhini loan, used almost exclusively over standard. |

| Near | దగ్గర | కాడ | kāḍa | Used for physical proximity (e.g., నా కాడ - near me). |
| Take | తీసుకో | తీస్కో | tīskō | Vowel elision in rapid speech/typing. |

### Required Rule Categories

**1. Pronoun System Differences**
Standard initial 'v' in 3rd person pronouns is frequently dropped in casual typing/speech (వాడు -> ఆడు, వాళ్ళు -> ఆళ్ళు). The standard feminine pronoun ఆమె (āme) is often pronounced and written as ఆమ (āma). The exclusive/inclusive "we" distinction is strictly maintained, but the forms are shortened (మేము -> మేం, మనము -> మనం).

**2. Copula and Auxiliary Forms**
The copula is mandatory and heavily used. The final vowels of standard forms are dropped.
*Affirmative Paradigm (ఉండు - to be):*
- 1sg: ఉన్న (unna)
- 2sg: ఉన్నావ్ (unnāv)
- 3sg masc: ఉండు / ఉన్నాడు (uṇḍu / unnāḍu)
- 3sg fem/neut: ఉంది (undi)
- 1pl: ఉన్నాం (unnāṃ)
- 3pl/2pl polite: ఉన్నారు (unnāru)
*Negative Paradigm (లేను - to not be):*
- 1sg: లేను (lēnu)
- 2sg: లేవు (lēvu)
- 3sg masc: లేడు (lēḍu)
- 3sg fem/neut: లేదు (lēdu)
- 1pl: లేము (lēmu)
- 3pl/2pl polite: లేరు (lēru)

**3. Past Tense Formation**
Standard Telugu uses *-ānu, -āvu, -āḍu* for past tense. Hyderabadi Telugu categorically uses *-ina, -inav, -iṇḍu*.
*Rule:* Replace Standard past suffix with Variety suffix.
*Standard:* వాడు అన్నం తిన్నాడు (vāḍu annaṃ tinnāḍu)
*Variety:* వాడు అన్నం తిన్నాడు -> వాడు అన్నం తినిండు (vāḍu annaṃ tiniṇḍu - He ate food).
*Standard:* నేను సినిమా చూశాను (nēnu sinimā cūśānu)
*Variety:* నేను సినిమా చూసిన (nēnu sinimā cūsina - I saw a movie).

**4. Perfect/Completive Tense**
Formed using the past participle of the verb + the present copula. This is used to indicate an action that has just been completed or whose effect continues.
*Pattern:* Verb stem + -i + copula.
- నేను చేసి ఉన్న (nēnu cēsi unna - I have done it)
- ఆడు పోయి ఉండు (āḍu pōyi uṇḍu - He has gone)
- మేం చెప్పి ఉన్నాం (mēṃ ceppi unnāṃ - We have told)

**5. Future Tense Formation**
Future tense is largely similar to Standard, but with shortened endings.
*Pattern:* Verb stem + -tā + personal ending.
- 1sg: చేస్తా (cēstā - I will do)
- 2sg: చేస్తావ్ (cēstāv - You will do)
- 3sg masc: చేస్తాడు (cēstāḍu - He will do)
- 3sg fem/neut: చేస్తది (cēstadi - She/it will do)

**6. Compound Verbs / Auxiliary Verb Constructions**
Verbs are frequently compounded to show completion, suddenness, or benefaction.
- **Completion (వెయ్ - vey / to throw):** Used to show an action is totally finished. 
  *Example:* తినెయ్ (tiney - eat it up), చేసేయ్ (cēsēy - do it completely).
- **Benefaction (పెట్టు - peṭṭu / to put):** Used when doing something for someone else.
  *Example:* రాసి పెట్టు (rāsi peṭṭu - write it down for me).
- **Self-action (కో - kō / from తీసుకో):** Used when doing something for oneself.
  *Example:* కొనుక్కో (konukkō - buy it for yourself).

**7. Negation**
Negation in the present continuous is highly distinct. Instead of standard (Verb + aḍaṃ + lēdu), this variety uses (Verb + ta + negative copula).
*Standard:* నేను చేయడం లేదు (nēnu cēyaḍaṃ lēdu)
*Variety:* నేను చేస్తలేను (nēnu cēstalēnu)
For simple past negation, it uses (Verb infinitive + lēdu) just like standard: నేను చేయలేదు (nēnu cēyalēdu).

**8. Question Formation**
- **Wh-questions:** Use the distinct lexical items: ఏడ (where), కైంకి/ఎందుకు (why), ఎట్ల (how - standard ఎలా), ఎవడు (who - informal masc).
- **Yes/No questions:** Marked by lengthening the final vowel of the verb, usually adding 'ā' (ఆ).
  *Example:* వస్తావా? (vastāvā? - Will you come?). 
  *Dual function note:* The word ఏం (ēṃ - what) is often used at the start of a sentence as a general conversational opener or yes/no prompt, similar to "so?". (e.g., ఏం, తిన్నావా? - So, did you eat?).

**9. Sentence-final Particles and Discourse Markers**
- **గదా (gadā):** "Isn't it? / Right?". Standard is కదా (kadā). Used constantly to seek agreement. (e.g., ఆడు మంచోడు గదా - He is a good guy, right?).
- **మల్ల (malla):** "Then / Again". Used as a filler or to mean "what else?". (e.g., మల్ల ఏంటి సంగతి? - Then what's the news?).
- **సరే గాని (sarē gāni):** "Okay, but...". Used to change the subject.
- **రా / బే (rā / bē):** Highly informal vocative particles used among close male friends. 'Bē' is a Dakhini loan. (e.g., ఏడ ఉన్నావ్ రా? - Where are you man?).

**10. Honorific/Address System**
- **Level 1 (Intimate/Inferior):** నువ్వు (nuvvu). Used with close friends, younger people, and AI assistants. Verbs take familiar endings.
- **Level 2 (Respectful/Formal):** మీరు (mīru). Used with elders, strangers, and in professional settings. Verbs take -andi/-ru endings.
- *Consequence of wrong choice:* Using 'nuvvu' with an elder is highly offensive. Using 'mīru' with a close friend sounds sarcastic or artificially distant.

---

## 3. Code-Mixing System

Code-mixing in Hyderabadi Urban Telugu is systematic and ubiquitous. It is triggered by domain (tech, corporate life, modern lifestyle), prestige (English sounds educated), and local identity (Dakhini Urdu sounds "street-smart" and authentic).

**Integration Grammar:**
- **English Verbs:** English verbs are almost never conjugated directly. They are borrowed as bare stems and paired with the Telugu auxiliary చెయ్ (cey - to do) or కొట్టు (koṭṭu - to hit/type). 
  *Example:* Call చెయ్ (Call cey), wait చేస్తన్నా (wait cēstannā).
- **English Nouns:** English nouns take Telugu case suffixes and plural markers seamlessly.
  *Example:* Office-ల (in the office), friends-తోని (with friends).
- **Script:** In native script typing, English words are usually transliterated into Telugu script (e.g., ఆఫీస్, కాల్, వెయిట్).

**Three Examples at Different Mixing Intensities:**

*Low Intensity (One English/Dakhini word):*
- Native Script: నేను రేపు ఆఫీస్ కి పోతలేను.
- Romanization: nēnu rēpu āphīs ki pōtalēnu.
- Gloss: I tomorrow office to going-not.
- Meaning: I am not going to the office tomorrow.

*Medium Intensity (English noun/verb phrase + Dakhini adjective):*
- Native Script: ఆడు ఫుల్ బిజీ ఉండు, డిస్టర్బ్ చేయకు.
- Romanization: āḍu phul bijī uṇḍu, ḍisṭarb cēyaku.
- Gloss: He full busy is, disturb do-not.
- Meaning: He is very busy, don't disturb him.

*High Intensity (English clause with native connective + Dakhini slang):*
- Native Script: దిమాక్ ఖరాబ్ అవుతుంది రా, I am totally exhausted అసలు, మల్ల కాల్ చేస్తా.
- Romanization: dimāk kharāb avutundi rā, I am totally exhausted asalu, malla kāl cēstā.
- Gloss: Brain bad happening man, I am totally exhausted actually, again call will-do.
- Meaning: My mind is messed up man, I am totally exhausted actually, I'll call you later.

---

## 4. AI Interaction Pattern

A typical Hyderabadi urban speaker treats an AI assistant as a useful, slightly formal tool, but addresses it using the informal "nuvvu" (you). The interaction is highly transactional and direct, often heavily mixed with English for technical or specific requests (e.g., "ఒక మంచి restaurant సజెస్ట్ చెయ్" - Suggest a good restaurant). If the AI misunderstands, the speaker will likely abandon Telugu and switch entirely to English, assuming the AI's local language capabilities are broken.

---

## 5. Three Authentic Chat Messages

**1. Casual/intimate (talking to a friend)**
- **Native Script:** ఏడ ఉన్నావ్ రా? జల్దీ రా, మస్తు ఆకలి అవుతుంది. ఈడ ట్రాఫిక్ ఫుల్ ఉంది.
- **Romanization:** (ēḍa unnāv rā? jaldī rā, mastu ākali avutundi. īḍa ṭrāphik phul undi.)
- **Standard Equivalent:** ఎక్కడ ఉన్నావు రా? త్వరగా రా, చాలా ఆకలిగా ఉంది. ఇక్కడ ట్రాఫిక్ ఎక్కువగా ఉంది.
- **Features Visible:** Lexical substitutions (ఏడ for ఎక్కడ, జల్దీ for త్వరగా, మస్తు for చాలా, ఈడ for ఇక్కడ). Use of informal vocative 'రా'.

**2. Slightly stressed/emotional (complaining to a colleague)**
- **Native Script:** నా తోని కాదు భయ్యా. పొద్దున సంది పని చేస్తన్న, దిమాక్ ఖరాబ్ అవుతుంది. మేనేజర్ కి చెప్పు నేను లాగ్ ఆఫ్ చేస్తున్న అని.
- **Romanization:** (nā tōni kādu bhayyā. podduna sandi pani cēstanna, dimāk kharāb avutundi. mēnējar ki ceppu nēnu lāg āph cēstunna ani.)
- **Standard Equivalent:** నా వల్ల కాదు సోదరా. ఉదయం నుండి పని చేస్తున్నాను, పిచ్చి ఎక్కుతోంది. మేనేజర్ కి చెప్పు నేను లాగ్ ఆఫ్ చేస్తున్నాను అని.
- **Features Visible:** Instrumental postposition (తోని instead of వల్ల), Ablative postposition (సంది instead of నుండి), Dakhini slang (దిమాక్ ఖరాబ్), Continuous verb form (చేస్తన్న instead of చేస్తున్నాను).

**3. Talking to an AI assistant (asking for practical help)**
- **Native Script:** నా లొకేషన్ కాడ మంచి బిర్యానీ ప్లేసెస్ ఏమున్నాయో వెతికి చెప్పు. రేటింగ్స్ బాగుండాలి.
- **Romanization:** (nā lokēṣan kāḍa mañci biryānī plēses ēmunnāyō vetiki ceppu. rēṭiṅgs bāguṇḍāli.)
- **Standard Equivalent:** నా లొకేషన్ దగ్గర మంచి బిర్యానీ ప్రదేశాలు ఏమున్నాయో వెతికి చెప్పు. రేటింగ్స్ బాగుండాలి.
- **Features Visible:** Postposition substitution (కాడ instead of దగ్గర), English plural integration (ప్లేసెస్ - places), direct imperative command to the AI (వెతికి చెప్పు).

---

## 6. What This Variety Does NOT Do

A common error when generating Hyderabadi Telugu is turning it into a caricature of Dakhini Urdu, completely abandoning Telugu grammar. While the vocabulary is heavily borrowed, the underlying syntax, verb conjugations, and sentence structure remain strictly Dravidian (Telugu). For instance, a speaker will say "కాల్ చేసిన" (kāl cēsina - I called), applying the Telugu past tense suffix to the Telugu auxiliary verb, rather than using Hindi/Urdu grammar.

Another mistake is applying Coastal Andhra slang (like *yāḍa*, *nāyana*, *bāgundā*) to a Hyderabadi persona. Hyderabadi Telugu does not use these; it uses *ēḍa*, *bhayyā/rā*, and *mastu undi*. Furthermore, while final vowels are often dropped in speech (e.g., *lēd* instead of *lēdu*), when typing in the native Telugu script, the script's syllabic nature often forces the vowel to be written (లేదు), even if the speaker reads it without the vowel. Over-engineering the script to look "broken" (like లేద్) is a telltale sign of inauthentic, forced dialect writing.

Here is the comprehensive production guide for the **Nellore-Prakasam Telugu** variety.

---

### 1. Character and Feel

Nellore-Prakasam Telugu represents the Southern Coastal Andhra dialect continuum. Geographically and linguistically, it serves as a bridge between the highly standardized, Sanskritized Telugu of the Krishna-Godavari delta to the north, the Rayalaseema dialect to the west, and shows faint traces of Tamil influence in the extreme south (Nellore). In text, it sounds deeply authentic, grounded, and slightly more relaxed or "drawled" than Standard Telugu. It lacks the sharp, rapid-fire clipping of urban Hyderabad Telugu and the heavy Urdu influence of Telangana.

Socially, this variety is the default community marker for both urban and rural speakers in these districts. It does not carry the "hyper-rural" stigma that media sometimes assigns to Rayalaseema or Srikakulam dialects, nor does it carry the elite prestige of Vijayawada/Guntur Standard. It is the language of everyday commerce, agriculture, and middle-class urban life in cities like Nellore and Ongole. Urban youth use it proudly in chat, often blending it with English, but retaining the distinct regional phonetic markers.

Phonologically, this variety is characterized by the dropping of word-initial 'v' in pronouns (e.g., *vaadu* becomes *aadu*), the transformation of spatial adverbs (e.g., *akkada* becomes *aada*), and the softening of certain consonants. In chat orthography, speakers often write exactly as they speak, dropping the formal sentence-final *-nu* (e.g., *vastunnaanu* is written as *vastunna*). Diglossia is present: speakers read and write formal Standard Telugu in academic settings, but their digital communication (WhatsApp, social media) strictly follows the spoken phonology.

---

### 2. Grammar: Production Rules

**General Transformation Rule:** To convert Standard Telugu to Nellore-Prakasam Telugu, drop formal literary suffixes (like final *-nu* and *-mu*), apply initial *v*-dropping on third-person pronouns, and replace standard spatial/locative adverbs with their regional equivalents.

#### Verb Paradigm Tables

**1. Motion Verb: "to go" (వెళ్ళు - vellu / పో - po)**
*Note: In this variety, 'po' is highly preferred for future/imperative, while 'ellu' (v-dropped 'vellu') is used for continuous/past.*

| Person | Standard | This Variety | Romanization |
|--------|----------|--------------|--------------|
| 1sg present | వెళ్తున్నాను | వెళ్తున్నా / ఎళ్తున్నా | veltunna / eltunna |
| 2sg familiar present | వెళ్తున్నావు | వెళ్తున్నావ్ / ఎళ్తున్నావ్ | veltunnav / eltunnav |

| 1sg past | వెళ్ళాను | వెళ్ళా / ఎళ్ళా / పోయా | vella / ella / poya |
| 3sg past | వెళ్ళాడు / వెళ్ళింది | వెళ్ళాడు / వెళ్ళింది (పోయాడు) | velladu / vellindi (poyadu) |

| Food/Meal | అన్నం భోజనం | అన్నం / బువ్వ | annam / buvva | 'Buvva' is used in intimate/family contexts. |

#### Required Rule Categories

1.  **Pronoun system differences:** The most prominent feature is the dropping of the initial 'v' in third-person pronouns in casual speech. *Vaadu* (he) becomes *aadu*; *vaallu* (they) becomes *aallu*. The accusative/dative forms follow suit (*vaadiki* -> *aadiki*).
2.  **Copula and auxiliary forms:** The copula drops the formal *-nu* and *-mu* endings.
    *   *Affirmative Paradigm:* 1sg: ఉన్నా (unna), 2sg: ఉన్నావ్ (unnav), 3sg(m): ఉన్నాడు (unnadu), 3sg(f/n): ఉంది (undi), 1pl: ఉన్నాం (unnam), 3pl/2pl(polite): ఉన్నారు (unnaru).
    *   *Negative Paradigm:* 1sg: లేను (lenu), 2sg: లేవు (levu), 3sg(m): లేడు (ledu), 3sg(f/n): లేదు (ledu), 1pl: లేము (lemu), 3pl/2pl(polite): లేరు (leru).
3.  **Past tense formation:** Formed by attaching the past suffix to the verb root, but strictly dropping the final *-nu* found in Standard Telugu.
    *   Standard: నేను తిన్నాను (nenu tinnaanu) -> Variety: నేను తిన్నా (nenu tinna - I ate).
    *   Standard: మేము వెళ్ళాము (memu vellaamu) -> Variety: మేం ఎళ్ళాం (mem em ellam - We went).
4.  **Perfect/completive tense:** Formed using the past participle of the main verb + the conjugated copula (ఉండు).
    *   Pattern: [Verb root + i] + [unna/unnadu/etc.]
    *   Example 1: నేను చేసి ఉన్నా (nenu chesi unna - I have done).
    *   Example 2: ఆడు ఎళ్ళి ఉన్నాడు (aadu elli unnadu - He has gone).
    *   Example 3: మేం చూసి ఉన్నాం (mem chusi unnam - We have seen).
5.  **Future tense formation:** Formed by adding *-ta* suffixes, dropping the final *-nu*.
    *   1sg: చేస్తా (chesta - I will do), 2sg: చేస్తావ్ (chestav), 3sg(m): చేస్తాడు (chestadu), 3sg(f/n): చేస్తది (chestadi - note: standard is చేస్తుంది chestundi, variety prefers చేస్తది chestadi), 1pl: చేస్తాం (chestam), 3pl: చేస్తారు (chestaru).
6.  **Compound verbs / auxiliary verb constructions:**
    *   **+ వెయ్ (vey - to put/throw):** Marks completion or suddenness. e.g., తినెయ్ (tiney - eat it up), పడేయ్ (padey - drop/throw it).
    *   **+ కొట్టు (kottu - to hit):** Used with English nouns to create verbs. e.g., కాల్ కొట్టు (call kottu - make a call), మెసేజ్ కొట్టు (message kottu - send a message).
    *   **+ కో (ko - reflexive/benefactive):** e.g., చూస్కో (chusko - watch out for yourself / see for yourself).
7.  **Negation:** The negative marker is లేదు (ledu) for objects/past actions, and కాదు (kaadu) for identity/equations. In present continuous, the suffix *-atle* is used instead of Standard *-atledu*.
    *   Example: నేను రావట్లే (nenu raavatle - I am not coming).
    *   Negative copula (full paradigm provided in Rule 2) is placed at the absolute end of the sentence.
8.  **Question formation:**
    *   *(a) Wh-questions:* Use regional interrogatives: యాడ (yaada - where), ఏంది (endi - what), ఎట్టా (etta - how).
    *   *(b) Yes/No questions:* Marked by elongating the final vowel to 'ఆ' (aa). e.g., వస్తావా? (vastavaa? - will you come?). 'ఏంటి' (enti) can also be tagged at the end to express surprise/questioning: వస్తావా ఏంటి? (vastavaa enti? - are you coming or what?).
9.  **Sentence-final particles and discourse markers:**
    *   **రా (raa):** Used with younger people or close male friends. e.g., ఇటు రా రా (itu raa raa - come here, man).
    *   **అబ్బా (abba):** Expresses mild frustration or emphasis. e.g., ఏందబ్బా ఇది (endabba idi - what is this, man!).
    *   **మరి (mari):** Used as "then" or "of course" at the end. e.g., వస్తా మరి (vasta mari - I will come then).
10. **Honorific/address system:** Two main levels.
    *   *Familiar/Inferior:* నువ్వు (nuvvu). Verbs take *-av* endings. Used for friends, younger siblings, subordinates.
    *   *Respectful/Formal:* మీరు (meeru). Verbs take *-aru* or *-andi* endings. Used for elders, strangers, and AI assistants. Using *nuvvu* with an elder is highly offensive.

---

### 3. Code-Mixing System

Code-mixing with English is highly productive and follows a systematic grammar, typical of educated urban and semi-urban speakers in Nellore and Prakasam.

**Trigger conditions:**
*   *Domain triggers:* Technology, education, and corporate work use English exclusively (e.g., "class", "system", "network").
*   *Formulaic triggers:* Greetings and apologies ("good morning", "sorry", "thanks", "actually").

**Integration grammar:**
*   English nouns take Telugu plural suffixes (e.g., బస్సులు - bussu-lu, క్లాసులు - class-lu).
*   English nouns take Telugu case markers (e.g., ఆఫీస్ కి - office-ki, బస్ లో - bus-lo).
*   English verbs are integrated using the auxiliary చెయ్ (chey - do) or కొట్టు (kottu - hit). (e.g., సెండ్ చెయ్ - send chey).
*   Script: English words are almost always transliterated into Telugu script when typing in Telugu, or the whole sentence is typed in Roman script (Manglish). For this guide's output, English words must be in Telugu script.

**Three examples at different mixing intensities:**

*   **Low (One English word):**
    *   Native script: నేను బస్ లో వస్తున్నా.
    *   Romanization: nenu bus lo vastunna.
    *   Gloss: I bus-in coming-am. (I am coming in the bus.)
*   **Medium (English noun/verb phrase):**
    *   Native script: ఆడ సిగ్నల్ లేదు, మళ్ళీ కాల్ చెయ్.
    *   Romanization: aada signal ledu, malli call chey.
    *   Gloss: There signal no, again call do. (There is no signal there, call me again.)
*   **High (English clause with native connective):**
    *   Native script: యాక్చువల్లీ నేను బిజీ గా ఉన్నా, బట్ రేపు మీట్ అవుదాం.
    *   Romanization: actually nenu busy gaa unna, but repu meet avudaam.
    *   Gloss: Actually I busy-as am, but tomorrow meet let's-become. (Actually I am busy, but let's meet tomorrow.)

---

### 4. AI Interaction Pattern

A typical speaker from this region treats an AI assistant as a helpful, slightly formal tool. They will likely use the respectful "మీరు" (meeru) register or neutral imperative forms, avoiding overly intimate markers like "రా" (raa). The interaction is direct and transactional. If the AI misunderstands, the speaker is likely to simplify their Telugu, drop regional slang (like *yaada* or *aada*), and switch to heavily English-mixed Standard Telugu, assuming the AI lacks local cultural context.

---

### 5. Three Authentic Chat Messages

**1. Casual/intimate (talking to a friend):**
*   **Message:** రేయ్, నేను బస్టాండ్ కాడ ఉన్నా. నువ్వు యాడ ఉన్నావ్? బిరాన రా, సినిమాకి టైమ్ అవుతుంది.
*   **Romanization:** (rey, nenu bus-stand kaada unna. nuvvu yaada unnav? biraana raa, cinema-ki time avutundi.)
*   **Standard equivalent:** ఒరేయ్, నేను బస్టాండ్ దగ్గర ఉన్నాను. నువ్వు ఎక్కడ ఉన్నావు? త్వరగా రా, సినిమాకి సమయం అవుతుంది.
*   **Features visible:** Use of *kaada* (near/at), *yaada* (where), *biraana* (quickly), dropping of final *-nu* in *unna/unnav*.

**2. Slightly stressed/emotional (complaining):**
*   **Message:** ఏందబ్బా ఈ కరెంటు కోతలు! పొద్దున్న నుంచి కరెంటు లేదు. ఇంట్ల చానా ఉక్కపోతగా ఉంది.
*   **Romanization:** (endabba ee current kotalu! poddunna nunchi current ledu. intla chaanaa ukkapotagaa undi.)
*   **Standard equivalent:** ఏమిటి అబ్బా ఈ విద్యుత్ కోతలు! ఉదయం నుండి విద్యుత్ లేదు. ఇంట్లో చాలా ఉక్కపోతగా ఉంది.
*   **Features visible:** *Endabba* (what is this, man - frustration marker), *intla* (locative *la* instead of *lo*), *chaanaa* (a lot/very), English loan "current" integrated natively.

**3. Talking to an AI assistant (asking for help):**
*   **Message:** నెల్లూరు లో రేపు వెదర్ ఎట్టా ఉంటది? వర్షం పడే ఛాన్స్ ఉందా?
*   **Romanization:** (nellore lo repu weather ettaa untadi? varsham pade chance undaa?)
*   **Standard equivalent:** నెల్లూరు లో రేపు వాతావరణం ఎలా ఉంటుంది? వర్షం పడే అవకాశం ఉందా?
*   **Features visible:** *Ettaa* (how - regional variant of *elaa*), *untadi* (3sg future instead of standard *untundi*), English loans "weather" and "chance" transliterated and integrated.

---

### 6. What This Variety Does NOT Do

**Common Errors & Over-applications:**
*   **No Telangana Slang:** The most common error AI makes is confusing all non-standard Telugu with Telangana Telugu. Nellore-Prakasam speakers do **NOT** use words like *mastu* (a lot), *giraaki* (customer/demand), *paisa* (money), or *kya* (what). Using these instantly ruins the coastal authenticity.
*   **No Hyper-Rayalaseema:** While they share some vocabulary, Nellore speakers do not typically use the heavy Rayalaseema intonation or specific markers like *biddalu* (for children generally) or *yaasa* heavily in text.
*   **Retained Standard Features:** They retain standard sentence structure and standard verb roots. A bad imitation tries to change the verb roots themselves (e.g., inventing new words for "eat" or "sleep"). The variety is mostly phonetic shortening and specific spatial adverbs (*aada, eeda, yaada*).
*   **Caricature Tells:** A bad imitation will overuse *biraana* (quickly) in every single sentence, or add *raa* to an AI assistant. Authentic speech uses regionalisms naturally and sparingly, heavily relying on English for modern concepts.

---

### 7. Persona Match Criteria

```yaml
persona_match:
  first_language: ["Telugu", "telugu"]
  state: ["Andhra Pradesh", "Andhra"]
  district: ["Nellore", "Sri Potti Sriramulu Nellore", "Prakasam", "Ongole", "Bapatla", "Kavali", "Gudur"]
  zone: ["Urban", "Semi-urban", "Rural"]
  education_level: ["High School", "Bachelors", "Masters", "Diploma", "Some College"]
  occupation_keywords: ["student", "farmer", "business", "software", "teacher", "clerk", "shop owner", "agriculture", "engineer"]
  cultural_background_signals: ["coastal andhra", "south coastal", "nellore", "ongole", "middle class", "telugu speaking"]
  linguistic_background_signals: ["coastal dialect", "nellore slang", "prakasam dialect", "manglish", "telugu with english mix"]
```

# Variety Characterization: Bambaiya Hindi

---

### 1. Character and Feel

Bambaiya Hindi (also known as Mumbaiya Hindi) is a vibrant, highly expressive contact vernacular spoken across the Mumbai Metropolitan Region, including Mumbai, Thane, and coastal urban Maharashtra. It developed as a lingua franca in the highly cosmopolitan, dense urban environment of Mumbai, drawing heavily from Marathi (the regional state language), Gujarati, Konkani, and English. Far from being a "broken" dialect, it is a highly systematic, rule-governed variety that serves as a powerful marker of local urban identity. It cuts across socio-economic classes; while historically associated with working-class neighborhoods and street culture, it is proudly used today by urban youth, college students, and professionals in informal digital communications to signal warmth, street-smart authenticity, and a shared "Mumbaikar" identity.

In written chat (such as WhatsApp or Instagram messages), Bambaiya Hindi has a distinct phonetic and rhythmic punch. It is characterized by the systematic shortening of vowels, the simplification of complex consonant clusters, and the complete neutralization of Standard Hindi's complex gender-agreement rules. In Devanagari script, speakers write phonetically to reflect their spoken accent—for instance, replacing the standard nasalized *nahīn* (नहीं) with *nai* (नइ) or *naai* (नाइ), and collapsing spatial deictics like *vahāṅ* (वहाँ) and *yahāṅ* (यहाँ) into *udhar* (उदर/उधर) and *idhar* (इदर/इधर). 

The variety is structurally diglossic. While speakers will use Standard Hindi or English for formal, academic, or professional writing, they switch instantly to Bambaiya Hindi for peer-to-peer chat. Code-mixing is a core feature of this variety: English nouns and verbs are seamlessly integrated using Hindi light-verb constructions, and Marathi grammatical structures (such as the experiential dative or specific aspectual markers) are mapped directly onto Hindi lexical items. The resulting text is fast-paced, highly colloquial, and deeply expressive.

---

### 2. Grammar: Production Rules

To transform Standard Hindi sentences into authentic Bambaiya Hindi, apply the following structural and lexical transformations:

#### Verb Paradigm Tables

##### 1. Motion Verb: जाना (To Go)

| Person | Standard | This Variety | Romanization |
|--------|----------|--------------|--------------|
| 1sg present | मैं जाता हूँ / जाती हूँ | मैं जाता है / अपुन जाता है | main jaata hai / apun jaata hai |
| 2sg familiar present | तू जाता है / जाती है | तू जाता है | tu jaata hai |
| 3sg present | वह जाता है / जाती है | वो जाता है | vo jaata hai |
| 1sg past | मैं गया / गयी | मैं गया / अपुन गया | main gaya / apun gaya |
| 3sg past | वह गया / गयी | वो गया | vo gaya |
| 1sg future | मैं जाऊँगा / जाऊँगी | मैं जाएगा / अपुन जाएगा | main jaega / apun jaega |
| 3sg future | वह जाएगा / जाएगी | वो जाएगा | vo jaega |
| 1sg negative present | मैं नहीं जाता हूँ | मैं नइ जाता / अपुन नइ जाता | main nai jaata / apun nai jaata |
| 3sg negative present | वह नहीं जाता है | वो नइ जाता | vo nai jaata |
| 2sg imperative | तू जा | तू जा / जा रे | tu ja / ja re |
| 2sg polite imperative | आप जाइए | तुम जाओ / आप जाओ | tum jao / aap jao |

##### 2. Modal/State Verb: होना (To Be / Happen)

| Person | Standard | This Variety | Romanization |
|--------|----------|--------------|--------------|
| 1sg present | मैं हूँ | मैं है / अपुन है | main hai / apun hai |
| 2sg familiar present | तू है | तू है | tu hai |
| 3sg present | वह है | वो है | vo hai |
| 1sg past | मैं था / थी | मैं था / अपुन था | main tha / apun tha |
| 3sg past | वह था / थी | वो था | vo tha |
| 1sg future | मैं हूँगा / हूँगी | मैं होएगा / अपुन होएगा | main hoega / apun hoega |
| 3sg future | वह होगा / होगी | वो होएगा | vo hoega |
| 1sg negative present | मैं नहीं हूँ | मैं नइ है / अपुन नइ है | main nai hai / apun nai hai |
| 3sg negative present | वह नहीं है | वो नइ है | vo nai hai |
| 2sg imperative | तू हो | तू हो / हो जा | tu ho / ho ja |
| 2sg polite imperative | आप होइए | तुम हो जाओ / आप हो जाओ | tum ho jao / aap ho jao |

##### 3. Action Verb: करना (To Do)

| 2sg familiar present | तू करता है / करती है | तू करता है | tu karta hai |
| 3sg present | वह करता है / करती है | वो करता है | vo karta hai |
| 1sg past | मैंने किया | मैं किया / अपुन ने किया | main kiya / apun ne kiya |
| 3sg past | उसने किया | वो किया / उसने किया | vo kiya / usne kiya |
| 1sg future | मैं करूँगा / करूँगी | मैं करेगा / अपुन करेगा | main karega / apun karega |
| 3sg future | वह करेगा / करेगी | वो करेगा | vo karega |
| 1sg negative present | मैं नहीं करता हूँ | मैं नइ करता / अपुन नइ करता | main nai karta / apun nai karta |
| 3sg negative present | वह नहीं करता है | वो नइ करता | vo nai karta |
| 2sg imperative | तू कर | तू कर / कर रे | tu kar / kar re |
| 2sg polite imperative | आप कीजिए | तुम करो / आप करो | tum karo / aap karo |

#### Pronoun Table

#### Key Lexical Substitutions

| Category | Standard | Variety | Romanization | Usage note |
|----------|----------|---------|--------------|------------|
| Water | पानी | पानी | paani | Same as standard, but never use high-register terms like 'जल'. |
| Good/fine | अच्छा / ठीक | कड़क / टकाटक | kadak / takatak | 'कड़क' means excellent/strong; 'टकाटक' means perfectly fine. |
| Child (boy) | लड़का | टेढ़ा / शेंबड़ा | tedha / shembda | 'शेंबड़ा' is a Marathi loanword used colloquially for a bratty kid. |
| Child (girl) | लड़की | पोरी / चिमणी | pori / chimni | 'पोरी' is directly from Marathi; highly common in informal speech. |
| Come | आना | टपकना | tapakna | Literally "to drip", used slangily for showing up unexpectedly. |
| Go | जाना | कल्टी मारना | kalti maarna | Used for leaving quietly, escaping, or flaking on plans. |
| What | क्या | क्या / काय | kya / kay | 'काय' is a Marathi loanword used frequently in rapid chat. |
| Why | क्यों | काइको | kaiko | Classic Bambaiya marker; highly diagnostic. |
| Here | यहाँ | इधर / इदर | idhar / idar | Standard 'यहाँ' is completely absent in casual chat. |
| There | वहाँ | उधर / उदर | udhar / udar | Standard 'वहाँ' is completely absent in casual chat. |
| Today | आज | आज | aaj | Same as standard. |
| Tomorrow | कल | कल | kal | Same as standard. |
| Now | अब | अभी | abhi | Standard 'अब' is replaced entirely by 'अभी'. |
| Home/house | घर | घर / खोपचा | ghar / khopcha | 'खोपचा' refers to a small room, corner, or private spot. |
| Person/man | आदमी | भिडू / पब्लिक | bhidu / public | 'भिडू' means friend/guy; 'पब्लिक' refers to people/crowd. |
| Want/Need | चाहिए | मंगता | mangta | Invariant modal; replaces 'चाहिए' in almost all contexts. |
| Problem/Mess | गड़बड़ / समस्या | लोचा / राड़ा | locha / rada | 'लोचा' is a mistake/problem; 'राड़ा' is a physical fight or big mess. |
| Smart/Clever | होशियार | शाणा | shaana | From Marathi 'शहाणा'; often used sarcastically. |
| Finished/Dead | खत्म | खल्लास | khallaas | Used for tasks completed, food finished, or things destroyed. |

---

#### Required Rule Categories

##### 1. Pronoun System
*   **Transformation**: Replace Standard first-person singular *main* (मैं) with *apun* (अपुन) to signal high informality, or retain *main* (मैं) but pair it with the invariant verb form *hai* (है).
*   **Dative/Accusative**: Never use *mujhe* (मुझे) or *tujhe* (तुझे). Always use *mere ko* (मेरे को) and *tere ko* (तेरे को).
*   **Plurals**: Append *log* (लोग) to pronouns to form plurals: *apun log* (अपुन लोग - we), *tum log* (तुम लोग - you all), *vo log* (वो लोग - they).

##### 2. Copula and Auxiliary Forms
Bambaiya Hindi simplifies the copula paradigm by neutralizing gender and number agreement. The verb defaults to the third-person singular form *hai* (है) or *tha* (था).

**Affirmative Copula Paradigm:**
*   1sg: मैं है / अपुन है (main hai / apun hai)
*   2sg familiar: तू है (tu hai)
*   2sg respectful: तुम है (tum hai)
*   3sg: वो है (vo hai)
*   1pl: हम लोग है / अपुन लोग है (hum log hai / apun log hai)
*   3pl: वो लोग है (vo log hai)

**Negative Copula Paradigm:**
*   1sg: मैं नइ है / अपुन नइ है (main nai hai / apun nai hai)
*   2sg familiar: तू नइ है (tu nai hai)
*   2sg respectful: तुम नइ है (tum nai hai)
*   3sg: वो नइ है (vo nai hai)
*   1pl: हम लोग नइ है / अपुन लोग नइ है (hum log nai hai / apun log nai hai)
*   3pl: वो लोग नइ है (vo log nai hai)

##### 3. Past Tense Formation
*   **Ergative Split Elimination**: Standard Hindi uses the agentive marker *ne* (ने) with transitive verbs in the past tense (e.g., *mainne bola*). In Bambaiya, *ne* is dropped for standard pronouns (*main bola* / मैं बोला) but is optionally retained in a non-standard way with *apun* (*apun ne bola* / अपुन ने बोला).
*   **Gender Neutralization**: Past tense verbs do not change for female subjects. A female speaker will say *main gaya tha* (मैं गया था) or *vo bhaag gaya* (वो भाग गया) instead of *main gayi thi* or *vo bhaag gayi*.

##### 4. Perfect/Completive Tense
*   **Pattern**: Formed by appending the auxiliary *daala* (डाला) or *diya* (दिया) directly to the verb root to emphasize absolute completion.
*   *Example 1*: Standard *mainne likh liya hai* -> Bambaiya *main likh dala* (मैं लिख डाला).
*   *Example 2*: Standard *usne kha liya* -> Bambaiya *vo kha dala* (वो खा डाला).
*   *Example 3*: Standard *mainne kaam kar diya* -> Bambaiya *apun kaam kar diya* (अपुन काम कर दिया).

##### 5. Future Tense Formation
*   **Pattern**: The future tense is formed by applying the suffix *-ega* (-एगा) to the verb root for all persons, genders, and numbers, completely bypassing standard agreement.
*   *1sg*: मैं जाएगा / अपुन जाएगा (main jaega / apun jaega)
*   *3sg*: वो जाएगा (vo jaega)
*   *1pl*: हम लोग जाएगा (hum log jaega)
*   *3pl*: वो लोग जाएगा (vo log jaega)

##### 6. Compound Verbs / Auxiliary Verb Constructions
*   **The 'Maarna' (मारना) Construction**: Used to denote performing an action quickly or casually.
    *   *kalti maarna* (कल्टी मारना) - to escape/leave.
    *   *palti maarna* (पलटी मारना) - to back out of a promise.
    *   *dandi maarna* (दांडी मारना) - to skip school/work.
*   **The 'Lene ka' (लेने का) / 'Dene ka' (देने का) Construction**: Influenced by Marathi grammar, expressing obligation or instruction.
    *   *tension nahi lene ka* (टेंशन नहीं लेने का) - Don't take tension.
    *   *usko de dene ka* (उसको दे देने का) - Just give it to him.

##### 7. Negation
*   **Marker**: Use *nai* (नइ / नाइ) instead of *nahīn* (नहीं).
*   **Placement**: *nai* is placed directly before the verb or at the very end of the clause for emphasis.
*   **Imperative Negation**: Use *nako* (नको - Marathi loan) or *mat* (मत). E.g., *load mat le* (लोड मत ले) or *tension nako le* (टेंशन नको ले).

##### 8. Question Formation
*   **Wh-Questions**: Interrogatives are placed in their standard positions but substituted with local forms: *kaiko* (काइको - why), *kya* (क्या - what), *kidhar* (किधर - where).
*   **Yes/No Questions**: Marked by appending the particle *kya* (क्या) or *na* (ना) to the very end of the sentence. E.g., *tu aa raha hai kya?* (तू आ रहा है क्या? - Are you coming?).

##### 9. Sentence-Final Particles and Discourse Markers
*   **रे (re) / ए (ey)**: Used to draw attention or add informal emphasis. E.g., *chal re* (चल रे), *ए भाई* (ey bhai).
*   **बॉस (boss) / भिडू (bhidu)**: Standard terms of address for peers.
*   **ना (na)**: Tag question or softener at the end of requests. E.g., *देना ना* (de na - please give).

##### 10. Honorific/Address System
*   The system is highly flattened. The formal *aap* (आप) is rarely used in casual chat; *tum* (तुम) or *tu* (तू) is the default even when speaking to older acquaintances or service providers. Respect is signaled through lexical markers like *boss* (बॉस) or *bhai* (भाई) rather than verbal honorific inflections.

---

### 3. Code-Mixing System

Bambaiya Hindi operates on a highly productive, rule-governed code-mixing system combining Hindi, Marathi, and English.

#### Trigger Conditions
*   **Domain Triggers**: Technology, work schedules, and modern urban lifestyle concepts trigger English words (e.g., *meeting*, *train*, *location*, *adjust*).
*   **Prestige/Expressive Triggers**: English adjectives are used to express emotional states (*tension*, *load*, *sorted*, *chill*). Marathi words are triggered for local cultural concepts, food, and emphatic exclamations (*rada*, *shembda*, *pori*).
*   **Formulaic Triggers**: Conversational fillers like *actually*, *by the way*, *sorry*, and *okay* are inserted seamlessly.

#### Integration Grammar
*   **English Verbs**: English verbs are integrated using the light verbs *karna* (करना - to do) or *hona* (होना - to be). The English verb remains in its base form. E.g., *manage karna* (मैनेज करना), *sort hona* (सॉर्ट होना).
*   **Case Suffixes**: Native postpositions attach directly to English nouns. E.g., *office mein* (ऑफिस में), *station pe* (स्टेशन पे).
*   **Script**: In native-script chats, English loanwords are transliterated into Devanagari phonetically (e.g., 'टेंशन', 'मैनेज', 'कॉल').

#### Examples of Mixing Intensity

##### Low Mixing
*   **Native Script**: आज ऑफिस में बहुत काम है यार।
*   **Romanization**: aaj office mein bahut kaam hai yaar.
*   **Gloss**: Today office in a-lot-of work is friend.

##### Medium Mixing
*   **Native Script**: तू सीन सॉर्ट कर ले, मैं टाइम पे पहुँच जाएगा।
*   **Romanization**: tu scene sort kar le, main time pe pahunch jaega.
*   **Gloss**: You situation resolve do take, I time on reach will-go.

##### High Mixing
*   **Native Script**: अपुन का बॉस बहुत मगजमारी करता है, फालतू में मीटिंग रख के पूरा संडे वेस्ट कर डाला।
*   **Romanization**: apun ka boss bahut magajmari karta hai, faaltu mein meeting rakh ke poora Sunday waste kar daala.
*   **Gloss**: My of boss a-lot-of annoyance does, uselessly in meeting keep having whole Sunday waste did completely.

---

### 4. AI Interaction Pattern

A typical speaker of Bambaiya Hindi interacts with an AI assistant as a highly functional, smart "jugaad" (workaround) partner rather than a formal authority figure. They maintain a highly casual, direct, and conversational tone, using imperative commands mixed with friendly local markers (e.g., "ए भाई, मेरे को दादर में कोई मस्त होटल बता ना"). They exhibit high digital literacy but low patience for overly formal, textbook-style responses. If the AI misunderstands, they do not switch to highly formal language; instead, they rephrase using simpler, direct Bambaiya-infused Hinglish or drop into English keywords to get the job done quickly.

---

### 5. Three Authentic Chat Messages

#### 1. Casual/Intimate (To a friend)
*   **Native Script**: अरे भाई, शाम को मिल रहा है क्या? अपुन लोग उधर ही कट्टा पे बैठेगा। तू कल्टी मत मार आज, बहुत टाइम से मिला नइ है।
*   **Romanization**: (are bhai, shaam ko mil raha hai kya? apun log udhar hi katta pe baithega. tu kalti mat maar aaj, bahut time se mila nai hai.)
*   **Standard Equivalent**: अरे भाई, शाम को मिल रहे हो क्या? हम लोग वहीं चबूतरे पर बैठेंगे। तुम आज मत भागना, बहुत समय से मिले नहीं हैं।
*   **Visible Features**: 
    *   *apun log* (अपुन लोग) for first-person plural.
    *   *katta* (कट्टा) - local slang for a hangout spot/ledge.
    *   *kalti mat maar* (कल्टी मत मार) - slang for "don't flake/escape".
    *   *nai* (नइ) for negation.

#### 2. Slightly Stressed/Emotional (Complaining about a situation)
*   **Native Script**: क्या लोचा हो गया यार, गाड़ी का टायर पंचर हो गया बीच रास्ते में। ऊपर से बारिश चालू है। तेरे को बोला था ना कल ही मैकेनिक को दिखा दे, पर तूने सुना नइ। अब बहुत लोड हो रहा है।
*   **Romanization**: (kya locha ho gaya yaar, gaadi ka tyre puncture ho gaya beech raaste mein. upar se baarish chaalu hai. tere ko bola tha na kal hi mechanic ko dikha de, par tune suna nai. ab bahut load ho raha hai.)
*   **Standard Equivalent**: क्या गड़बड़ हो गई यार, गाड़ी का टायर रास्ते के बीच में पंचर हो गया। ऊपर से बारिश शुरू हो गई है। तुमसे कहा था ना कल ही मैकेनिक को दिखा दो, पर तुमने सुना नहीं। अब बहुत चिंता हो रही है।
*   **Visible Features**:
    *   *locha* (लोचा) for problem/mess.
    *   *baarish chaalu hai* (बारिश चालू है) - Marathi-influenced aspectual construction.
    *   *tere ko* (तेरे को) for second-person dative.
    *   *load ho raha hai* (लोड हो रहा है) - English loan used for stress.

#### 3. Talking to an AI Assistant (Asking for practical help)
*   **Native Script**: ए भाई, मेरे को दादर स्टेशन के पास कोई अच्छा और सस्ता खाने का जगह बता ना। ज्यादा महंगा नहीं मंगता, एकदम कड़क वड़ापाव या मिसल मिलना चाहिए। जल्दी बोल!
*   **Romanization**: (ey bhai, mere ko Dadar station ke paas koi achha aur sasta khaane ka jagah bata na. zyada mehnga nahi mangta, ekdam kadak vadapav ya misal milna chahiye. jaldi bol!)
*   **Standard Equivalent**: भाई, मुझे दादर स्टेशन के पास कोई अच्छा और सस्ता खाने का स्थान बताइए। ज्यादा महंगा नहीं चाहिए, बहुत बढ़िया वड़ापाव या मिसल मिलना चाहिए। जल्दी बताइए!
*   **Visible Features**:
    *   *ey bhai* (ए भाई) - casual address to the AI.
    *   *mere ko* (मेरे को) for first-person dative.
    *   *mangta* (मंगता) - invariant modal for "want/need".
    *   *kadak* (कड़क) - slang for excellent/strong.

---

### 6. What This Variety Does NOT Do

When generating Bambaiya Hindi, avoid the common pitfalls of caricature (often seen in older Bollywood movies depicting "tapori" characters). 

1.  **Do not overuse "अपुन" (apun) to the exclusion of "मैं" (main)**: Real speakers use both. Over-applying *apun* in every single sentence sounds unnatural and parodic.
2.  **Do not append "रे" (re) or "साला" (saala) to every clause**: These are emphatic markers used sparingly for emotional punctuation, not default sentence endings.
3.  **Do not use outdated slang**: Words like *jhakaas* (झकास) are highly dated and rarely used by modern urban youth in text messages; contemporary speakers prefer *kadak* (कड़क), *sahi hai* (सही है), or *solid* (सॉलिड).
4.  **Do not drop all grammar**: Bambaiya Hindi is simplified, but it is not random. It retains strict word order (Subject-Object-Verb) and systematic Marathi-influenced structural rules.

---

### 7. Persona Match Criteria

```yaml
persona_match:
  first_language: ["Hindi", "Marathi", "Gujarati", "Urdu"]
  state: ["Maharashtra"]
  district: ["Mumbai", "Mumbai Suburban", "Thane", "Palghar", "Raigad"]
  zone: ["Urban"]
  education_level: ["Secondary", "Higher Secondary", "Graduate", "Post Graduate"]
  occupation_keywords: ["student", "engineer", "executive", "driver", "freelancer", "agent", "shopkeeper", "delivery"]
  cultural_background_signals: ["Mumbaikar", "local train commuter", "coastal Maharashtra", "cosmopolitan urban"]
  linguistic_background_signals: ["bilingual Marathi-Hindi", "multilingual", "Hinglish speaker", "Mumbai slang user"]
```

# Variety Characterization Prompt: Medinipuria (South-Western Bengali)

## 1. Character and Feel

Medinipuria (also known as South-Western Bengali or Kanthi/Contai Bengali) is a distinct and vibrant variety of Bengali spoken primarily in the Purba Medinipur (East Midnapore) and parts of Paschim Medinipur (West Midnapore) districts of West Bengal, near the Odisha border. Because of its geographical location, it serves as a linguistic bridge between Standard Colloquial Bengali (SCB) and Odia, absorbing vocabulary, intonation patterns, and morphological traits from both, while maintaining its own unique identity. 

In text, particularly in informal digital communication like WhatsApp or Facebook, Medinipuria sounds earthy, direct, and highly phonetic. Speakers often bypass standard Bengali orthography to spell words exactly as they pronounce them. Phonologically, the most striking feature is the vowel shift: the Standard Bengali *o-kar* (ো) frequently shifts to *u-kar* (ু) (e.g., *bhalo* becomes *bhalu*), and *e-kar* (ে) often shifts to *i-kar* (ি) or *a-kar* (া) (e.g., *keno* becomes *kine*). There is also a distinct rhythm to the sentence structure, heavily punctuated by the affectionate and ubiquitous sentence-final particle "গো" (go).

Socially, Medinipuria carries a strong sense of community pride, though it has historically been stigmatized as "rural" or "unrefined" in elite Kolkata circles. Today, urban youth and migrants from Medinipur living in Kolkata or other cities use it playfully and deliberately in chats as an in-group solidarity marker. It is not a parody dialect; it is the authentic, everyday voice of millions. 

A strict diglossia exists: speakers will write formal emails, school essays, and official documents in perfect Standard Bengali, but will immediately switch to phonetic Medinipuria when texting friends and family. Code-mixing with English is common among the educated youth, but English loanwords are often seamlessly integrated into the local morphological system (e.g., taking Medinipuria case markers or verb endings).

---

## 2. Grammar: Production Rules

To convert Standard Colloquial Bengali (SCB) into Medinipuria, apply the following transformation rules.

### Verb Paradigm Tables

| 2sg familiar present | যাস | যাস | jas |
| 3sg present | যায় | যায় | jay |
| 1sg past | গেলাম | গেনু | genu |
| 3sg past | গেলো | গেলা | gela |
| 1sg future | যাবো | যাব | jabo / jab |

**3. Action Verb: "to do" (করা)**
